entries.prompt: What's something you've been putting off lately — not a chore, but a conversation or decision? Describe the last time it crossed your mind: what were you doing, and what did you do with the thought when it showed up?
entries.content:
Was on the bus this morning, headphones in, not really listening to anything — I'd just hit shuffle and then forgot to care about what came on. Someone got on at the 14th Street stop and sat two rows up, and something about the back of their head looked like Ben. Same haircut or something. It wasn't Ben. Obviously it wasn't Ben. Ben is four states away eating brunch at cool restaurants and posting about it like we're all supposed to feel fine about that. But for a second I felt this lurch in my chest, the kind where your body gets excited before your brain catches up, and I thought *oh*, and then the brain arrived and said *no*, and I spent the rest of the ride looking out the window thinking about the phone call I've been meaning to make to him for probably six weeks now.

Not a text. A call. That's the thing I keep not doing.

The last real conversation I had with Ben — like actually talking, not just reacting to things in the group chat — was sometime in February I think. Maybe late January. He was going through something with his job situation and I remember I was walking around my block at night because I couldn't get comfortable in the apartment, and we talked for almost an hour. I remember thinking afterward, okay, that's the thing, that's what we're supposed to be doing, that's the real version. And then I just. Didn't do it again. Six weeks of group chat where we send each other links and memes and Nat does the thing where she screenshots tweets about wine and Priya sends voice memos that are honestly too long but I love them anyway, and it all feels like friendship if you don't look too closely at what's actually being said.

The thought on the bus was: call him tonight.

What I actually did with the thought was put it somewhere in the pile of other thoughts and then started reading an article I didn't finish and then got to work.

And I'm writing about it now at 11:15 so I guess I haven't fully buried it.

The thing I don't totally understand is why I'm putting it off. There's nothing hard about it. Ben is one of my actual favorite people. It's not like the call with my parents where I have to spend the first twenty minutes fielding questions and then manufacture reassurance about money and then hang up feeling like I've been mildly wrung out. Ben is not like that. When Ben and I talk we usually end up laughing about something stupid and then saying something real at the end, right before we hang up, like we save it for last. That's not a reason to avoid something. That's a reason to want it.

But there's this thing. This quiet thing. Where I wonder if enough time has passed that we've both changed a little and the call won't feel like the call in February. I wonder if I'll pick up the phone and realize that the person I think of as Ben, the one I carry around in my chest, has become slightly different from the actual Ben. Not worse. Just. That maybe we've both been becoming people and we haven't been watching it happen together. And if I call him I'll have to find out whether the gap is small or bigger than I think. It's easier to keep the Ben in my chest exactly where he is.

*That's a terrible reason not to call someone.* I know that.

But it's the real one.

I think about the trip. Every few months someone says we should do a trip, everyone agrees, and then nothing. The thread goes quiet and then two weeks later Priya sends a voice memo about something else and we let it drop again. At some point I stopped being the one who pushed for it. I don't know exactly when. There was a version of me in college who was the person who made things happen, who sent the first text, who booked the thing. I liked that person. I don't know where they went or if they just got tired or if moving somewhere new alone kind of knocked the momentum out of you in ways you don't fully feel until you notice the absence.

Avery asked me last week, in this very casual way, about whether I had plans to see any of them soon. Not pushy. Just curious. And I said something vague like "we've been talking about a trip" and Avery did this small nod and didn't say anything else, which might mean nothing, but my brain ran with it for about half an hour afterward. *You don't actually do the trip. You don't make the call. You just coast on the idea of the friendship and hope nobody checks.* That's what my brain said. My brain is not always generous.

Or maybe it's accurate. Maybe that's the worse option.

I'm going to be honest about something. Part of it is that talking to Ben, or Nat, or Priya — actually talking — reminds me of the version of myself from before. Before this city and this apartment and this job where I'm still not sure what I'm doing most of the time. Before I became the person who gets on the bus and doesn't know what he's listening to. The college version was messy too but the mess felt temporary, like it was on the way to something. This mess feels more like. A climate. I don't want to explain that on a phone call. I don't want to perform being fine and I also don't want to be the person who is not fine in a way that makes everyone feel weird.

Ben would probably not feel weird. Ben would probably say something that made me laugh and then say something real at the end.

Okay. The bus tomorrow is about twenty-two minutes. I could call him from the bus. That's the plan. I'm writing it down so it's somewhere outside my head and not just another thought I put in the pile.

The plant on the windowsill is looking better actually. I don't know what I did. I just started paying slightly more attention to it and now there are two new leaves. Make of that what you will.

entries.prompt: What's something you said recently that came out harsher than you meant it to — or kinder than you actually felt? Walk me through the moment: what was the situation, what did you say, and what were you actually thinking?
entries.content:
Thursday. On the bus home, 6:40-something, sandwiched between a woman with a very large tote bag and a guy listening to music loud enough that I could identify the song. I kept replaying a thing I said to Reese this afternoon and by the time I got to my stop I'd replayed it maybe eleven times.

Context: we're both working on this thing for Drew, adjacent parts of it, and Reese sent me a version of a document in the early afternoon with a note that was polite and collaborative and honestly fine. And I read it and the first thing I thought was *oh this is actually better than mine.* Cleaner. The structure was cleaner. I spent like three days on my version and Reese had turned this out and it was just — tidier. That's not a performance of modesty, that's what happened in my chest, which was: a clench. A quick involuntary clench.

What I said, in the Slack response, was: *"This is great, really clean structure — I think yours actually works better for what Drew's looking for, mine got a little complicated in the middle. Combining might be the move."*

Kinder than I felt? Yes. But also — it's complicated.

Because I meant it. The thing is I actually meant it. It was better. So what I said was true. But the *tone* of it, the *ease* of it — that was manufactured. In reality I'd typed three different versions of the response before landing on that one. Version one was very neutral and mentioned combining without saying his was better. Version two was a little too complimentary in a way I worried would read as sarcastic. Version three was what I sent, and I spent probably eight minutes on those two sentences. Eight minutes to sound unbothered about something that bothered me, and the bothering was so stupid, so small, and yet I couldn't just — type a normal thing and send it.

And here's the part I keep snagging on: I don't even think I'm threatened by Reese exactly. I don't think I dislike Reese. Reese is fine, is actually pretty decent, brings food back for the whole team sometimes without making a thing of it. It's not about Reese. It's about this specific sensation of someone your age doing something slightly better than you and your whole nervous system treating it like incoming fire.

*It was one document. One section of one document. You have done good work. This is not a verdict.*

I know. I know that.

But the other part of my brain, the loud part, was doing the math all afternoon. Reese started six months before me. Okay. But if in six months I'm not making documents like that, what does that mean. Drew is going to compare them, obviously Drew is going to compare them, that's literally part of Drew's job, and what is Drew going to think, and does Drew already think that, and how long has Drew been thinking it, and when did Reese just — become someone who thinks in clean structures like that, and why is my default mode to make things complicated in the middle.

I make things complicated in the middle. That tracks, actually. That's not just a document problem.

The harsher thing came later and it wasn't at work.

Avery called around 7, while I was still in this specific mood, still processing the Reese thing without knowing I was processing it. And Avery asked how my day was, which is a completely normal question. And I said, "Fine, long, whatever." Which: fine and long, both possibly true. Whatever. That word. I heard it come out and I knew immediately it was wrong. Not cruel exactly but — clipped. Dismissive. Avery made this small pause and then said "okay, do you want to talk later" and I said "no it's fine, I'm fine" and I made myself unfold a little and we talked for maybe fifteen minutes about Avery's day which was actually more eventful than mine and I laughed at a couple of things and it was okay by the end.

But the *whatever.* That was me taking something out on someone who had nothing to do with it. That was me being too full of something and not knowing where to put it and then pointing the runoff in the wrong direction.

I wasn't going to mention the Reese thing. And I didn't, and that's probably fine, it's not really a thing to tell, but there was this stretch in the conversation where Avery was being warm and easy and I thought about saying: *I spent eight minutes writing two sentences so I could seem like I don't care about something I clearly care about, and then I couldn't stop doing the math the rest of the day, and now I'm here and I don't know how to just be fine yet.* And I didn't say it. Partly because it's embarrassing. Partly because I wasn't sure Avery would get it, which might be unfair, Avery probably would get it, Avery is patient in a way that I keep failing to account for. But partly because I didn't want to be the person who comes home (comes to the phone) still damp from a Tuesday-level work thing. I want to be the person who shakes that off. I want to be easy to be with.

The *whatever* was the price of trying to be easy to be with. It came out before I could manage it.

I thought about calling Ben from the bus this morning. I didn't. It was raining and the bus was crowded and I told myself I'd do it tonight and now it's 11:03 and I'm not going to call anyone at 11:03. So that's where that is. The plan just rolled twenty-four hours and I'm aware that's a pattern.

The thing about the Slack message though. I think there's something almost more unsettling about saying something kinder than you feel than saying something harsher. Because at least when you're harsh, something real leaked out. The performed kindness is you deciding what you want to look like and then producing that. And I was good at it today. I sounded generous and clear and like someone who can take a hit and keep their head up. Reese sent back a thumbs up and then a few thoughts on combining. Drew saw the thread and said "good collaboration, let's pull this into a meeting tomorrow."

Good collaboration.

Great. Excellent. 

I'm going to eat the rest of the pad thai in the fridge and not think about whether that's what I actually want or just what's there.

The plant has a third new leaf. I keep waiting to find out I'm doing something wrong that will catch up with me.

entries.prompt: What's something you agreed to recently that you later wished you hadn't — walk me through the moment you said yes.
entries.content:
The yes happened in a conference room at 2:15 on a Wednesday and I keep wishing I could go back and watch myself say it from the outside. Like play the tape. See my own face.

Drew had pulled together this informal thing — four of us around the table, not a real meeting, more like a standing-around-with-coffee situation that turned into a meeting. Something about a new workstream, early stages, and Drew was going around the table doing that thing where he's not exactly asking for volunteers but he's also completely asking for volunteers. Describing pieces of it, leaving pauses. Reese said something measured and smart about capacity. The other person, Amara, said she'd want to see the scope document before committing. And then it came around to me and I heard myself say "yeah, I can take point on that part, I've got room for it."

I don't have room for it.

I knew I didn't have room for it. I knew it the way you know you shouldn't order the thing you're about to order. But Drew had this look, this settled nod when I said it, like *good, that's useful, I can count on that* — and I felt the thing that feeling produces in me which is an immediate, almost physical relief, like I'd passed something. And then the meeting ended and I walked back to my desk and sat down and thought: what did you just do.

The part that got me doing the math in the elevator: Reese had said she'd want to see more before committing. Measured. Smart. And I said yes immediately, like I was grateful to be asked.

*You volunteered. Nobody was even looking at you specifically and you volunteered. Because Drew nodded at you and you needed the nod.*

I know why I did it. I know exactly why I did it and I can barely stand to write it because it's so transparent when I look at it flat: I wanted Drew to think I was someone with capacity. Someone unflappable. Someone who doesn't get in the weeds or make things complicated in the middle, and I know I said that before, I know that's a pattern I keep snapping back to. I wanted to look like more than I feel like right now. And the cost of that is I said yes to something I need to now actually do, with time I don't actually have.

It's not even that the thing is terrible. It's probably fine. I'll figure it out. I always figure it out in the sense that I work too late and eat bad food and eventually something acceptable lands on Drew's desk and it's okay. That's the pattern. That's not exactly thriving, that's just managing to look like thriving from a certain distance.

I texted Avery around 4 to say I'd be late tonight and Avery said okay and asked if I wanted Avery to grab anything. And I said no, I'm fine, I'll grab something. Which I did. I grabbed a burrito from the place on the corner and ate half of it over my keyboard and the other half on the bus at 8:30 and now it's 11:30 and I'm at the kitchen table and the plate from yesterday's takeout is still in the sink because I didn't deal with that either.

Avery came over and we watched something for about an hour and I was physically present but mentally still back in the conference room, still watching myself say *I've got room for it* with that eager, calm, total lie of a voice. Avery fell asleep on the couch with about fifteen minutes left in the episode and I just let it play and looked at the side of Avery's face and thought something that I wasn't expecting to think which was: Avery would have said no. Not meanly. Just — would have said not right now, let me look at the scope first, the thing Amara said. Avery is constitutionally able to do that and I genuinely don't know if that's a skill or just a confidence I can't locate in myself. Maybe both.

I thought about waking Avery up to tell them about it. The yes, the nod, the whole dynamic. I didn't because it was late and also because I've been coming to Avery with a lot of work stuff and I can feel myself pushing against some limit I can't see clearly. Not Avery's limit. Some limit I have about how much I want to need someone. About whether needing them that much is the same as being too much. I do this, I know I do this. I get close to saying the real thing and then I hear some alarm that says *careful* and I put it away.

The Avery-sleeping thing actually settled something in me a little. Just watching someone sleep, the breathing, the whole absence of performance. Nobody is trying to seem like anything. I sat there on the couch for probably ten minutes not thinking about Drew or the workstream or Reese's measured and smart non-commitment, just watching Avery breathe. That felt like something. I don't know what to do with it but it felt like something.

I did call Ben last week. Thursday night, made myself do it. It was good. I knew it would be good. We talked for almost an hour and he's going through some stuff with his apartment situation and I told him about the job thing in a pretty surface way, not the whole texture of it, but enough, and at the end he said you seem more settled than when we last talked, which I think was meant to be a compliment and also made me feel weirdly sad. More settled. Yeah. That's one way to say it.

The plant has leveled off. Not getting worse. Not growing either. Just maintaining, just being a plant that is alive, and that's maybe enough for the plant right now.

I want to go to sleep but I know I'm going to lie there and replay the conference room. *I've got room for it.* The nod. Reese in my peripheral vision. The elevator afterward when I asked myself what I'd just done and didn't have a good answer.

Someday I want to be the kind of person who waits for the scope document. Not in a scared way. Just in a — I know what I have and I know what I don't way. That sounds incredibly simple. I have no idea how to do it.

entries.prompt: When's the last time you laughed at something and then felt a little strange about it afterward — like the laugh surprised you, or didn't quite fit the moment? Walk me through it.
entries.content:
Friday at lunch, in the kitchen at work. The one with the bad overhead light that makes everyone look slightly unwell. Reese was heating something up and I was waiting for the microwave and someone from another team — I don't even know her name, she sits near the windows — was telling a story about her upstairs neighbor. Something about a Roomba. The Roomba had gotten loose in the middle of the night and driven itself into a door repeatedly for forty minutes before she woke up, and she was doing this impression of lying there in the dark trying to identify the sound, thinking someone was breaking in, progressively more convinced she was about to be murdered, and then turning on the light and seeing this little disc just bumping into the door over and over like it had a grudge.

I laughed. A lot. Harder than I probably should have for a story about a stranger's robot vacuum. It just caught me somewhere genuine — the image of it, the determination of the thing, the way she told it with her whole face. Reese was laughing too and even the guy who never talks to anyone, Marcus, was smiling into his tupperware. And for like sixty seconds the kitchen felt actually good. One of those little pockets of okayness where you forget to monitor anything.

And then we all kind of drifted out. The woman went back to her desk. Reese rinsed something in the sink. I took my food and went back to my corner and sat down.

The strange feeling arrived about four minutes later while I was eating and looking at the workstream document I said yes to on Wednesday. Just this quiet weird thing in the chest. Not guilt exactly. Not regret. Something more like: *that was the most naturally I've laughed in a while.* With coworkers. With people I see every day and still feel like I'm performing slightly around. And the naturalness of it was almost unsettling. Like finding a window in a room you'd accepted didn't have windows.

*Why can't you just be like that. Why is that the exception and not the state of things.*

Which is such an exhausting question to ask yourself after laughing at a story about a Roomba.

I think what I'm actually annoyed about is this: I'm good at managing what I put out at work. The Slack messages that take eight minutes to sound effortless, the yes in the conference room, the document thing with Reese. All managed. And somehow when I'm managing so hard it becomes hard to remember what it feels like to just — react to something. Just laugh because something was funny. The moment in the kitchen was real because I wasn't ready for it. Nobody told me it was coming. I couldn't prep a version of my laugh to send.

And then I sat back down at my desk and felt vaguely sad about laughing at a Roomba story.

I told Avery about it last night. The story, I mean — the Roomba, the impression, the whole thing. And Avery laughed too, which was good, and I realized I was performing it a little, doing the impression second-hand, trying to reconstruct the kitchen feeling on the couch. It landed, Avery was genuinely laughing, and I felt something that was partly good and partly this additional layer of the weird thing. Because I'd taken this moment of accidental realness and then packaged it. Carried it home and presented it. Here, look, I can be funny and light, look at the funny thing from my day.

*Why are you like this. She's your person. Just tell her it was a good moment.*

I didn't say that. I made Avery laugh and we moved on and the evening was fine. More than fine, actually. We ordered from the Thai place and Avery picked a movie I would never have picked and it was one of those nights that's just comfortable in a way that feels like something you could get used to. The kind of comfortable where you're not thinking about Drew or the workstream or whether Reese files things in a mental structure that's tidier than yours. Avery fell asleep again before it ended. I stayed up another hour just — being in the apartment. My apartment. With this person in it, asleep on my couch.

I thought about the Roomba bumping into the door in the dark. The determination of it. Just doing its one thing, no concept of the obstacle.

I don't know. There's something I want to say about that but I'm not sure I have it.

The Ben call is still sitting okay in me. The thing he said — *you seem more settled* — I've been turning it over. I think I decided it bothers me because it's not wrong. I am more settled in the surface sense. Less visibly chaotic than I was at 22. But settled feels like the wrong word for what's actually happening, which is more like: I've gotten better at maintaining the shape. The inside still moves around. The inside is still a conference room at 2:15, still eight minutes spent on two sentences, still a kitchen with a bad overhead light where I was briefly and accidentally myself for sixty seconds and then had to go process it.

I haven't said any of this to Avery in any kind of complete way. I talk around it. I bring the Roomba story, not the part about sitting back down at my desk and feeling the small strange thing. I say *work is a lot* but I don't say *I said yes to something I shouldn't have said yes to because I needed Drew to nod at me like I was worth something.* I get close to the real version and then I hear the alarm and redirect.

What I want — and I'm just going to write it flat and not clean it up — is to be a person Avery knows. Not the managed version. Not the one who makes things complicated in the middle and then spends eight minutes sounding like he doesn't. The one in the kitchen laughing at a Roomba. I want that person to be the main one, not the occasional one. I don't know how to get from here to there. I think it requires saying things that feel too unformed to say.

Maybe that's not true. Maybe Avery already knows more than I think and I'm the one still pretending there's something to hide.

The plant has a fourth leaf. Small. Coming in from the side. I don't know what it's doing or why it picked now but I'm not going to question it.

entries.prompt: Describe a moment outside of work this week when you felt completely at ease — what were you doing, who (if anyone) was around, and what made it feel different from the rest of the day?
entries.content:
Saturday morning. Avery was still asleep and I'd gotten up early, not on purpose, just one of those times where your body decides it's done before your brain agrees. I stood in the kitchen in socks and made coffee and the whole apartment had this quality of light that only happens before anyone has needed anything from it. Gray-blue. Quiet in a way that doesn't feel like waiting.

I stood at the window with the mug and watched the street for a while. A man walking a dog that kept stopping. A delivery bike. Someone in a puffy coat moving fast with somewhere specific to be, and I felt, watching that person, the particular luxury of not having somewhere to be.

That's the thing. That's when it was different.

Because the rest of the week everything had somewhere to be. The workstream thing has started in earnest — Drew set the first checkpoint for two weeks out, which I found out on a calendar invite with no other context, just the date sitting there, and I've been carrying the weight of it like something strapped to my back that I keep trying to forget is there. Every time I get a few hours of not-thinking-about-it I'll be in the bathroom or on the elevator and it drops back down. The nod in the conference room. *I've got room for it.* Great job, me.

But Saturday morning with the coffee and the window, I wasn't in the conference room. I was just. There.

I heard Avery moving around eventually — the specific sound of feet on the floor, the creak by the bathroom door that I've learned to recognize as the sound of someone who'll be up in five minutes. I poured a second mug and waited, which was not a strategic thing I did, I just did it without thinking, and when Avery came in, still in the big gray sweatshirt, hair doing its own thing, I held out the mug and Avery took it without saying anything and we just stood next to each other at the counter.

No phones. Not a rule, just how it happened.

We stayed like that for probably ten minutes. Drank coffee. Avery looked out the window. I looked at the plant, which now has four leaves and is apparently just doing that, just adding leaves. Sometimes one of us said something and sometimes we didn't. Avery made a small sound at a pigeon on the fire escape, like half a laugh, and I looked and the pigeon was doing an extremely confident walk across the railing and we both watched it and then it left.

That's the whole story.

And I'm writing about it now because when I try to think about when I felt at ease this week that's the only moment that comes up without any other feeling attached to it. It was just its own thing. No alarm, no eight-minute draft of how to seem, no math about what it meant. Avery with the coffee and the pigeon and the gray-blue light and the street below still mostly empty. That was it.

What I don't totally know how to account for is that it wasn't complicated. It was so not complicated. And I spend such a large amount of my time making things complicated in the middle — I know I keep saying that, it keeps being true — that when something is just *simple and good* I almost don't know where to put it. I keep waiting for the catch. The thing where the ease reveals itself to be slightly fake.

It didn't. We drank the coffee. Avery made eggs eventually and I made toast, slightly too dark the way I always make it, and we ate standing up because neither of us had moved to sit down and it felt too late to decide to. Avery teased me about the toast. I said something back that I've already forgotten but made Avery laugh. This easy domestic thing that I have no idea when it became available to me.

I thought later, on the bus to get groceries — Avery needed to do something else, we split up, I had the list in my phone — about the entry I wrote where I was watching Avery sleep on the couch and thinking about how nobody performs while they're asleep. Saturday morning was like that but both of us. Both of us not performing, not managing, just making coffee in a kitchen and watching a pigeon.

*Why can't that be the main thing.* I know I keep asking this, in different shapes. The Roomba story, the couch, now this. I keep stumbling into pockets of being okay and then climbing back out and wondering how to live in them instead of just visiting.

I think about that and I don't have an answer. I have the observation, and then a bunch of space after it.

The thing I'm aware of is that I didn't tell Avery. Didn't say: that was something, this morning. That was the best I've felt all week and it was doing nothing with you for ten minutes. Which is a very simple thing to say. Avery would have — I don't know, looked at me, maybe smiled in that specific quiet way. It would have been fine. It would have probably been good. And I kept not saying it and we moved into the rest of Saturday and now it's Sunday night and the window for saying it has a certain feeling of passed.

I could still say it. That's not a window that actually closes. I just let myself think it does and then I don't have to deal with whatever comes next, which is probably nothing, probably just someone knowing a true thing about me.

The workstream document is open in another tab right now and I need to put some actual hours into it tomorrow and I've been not-looking at it for about an hour and a half. That's fine. Tonight I'm letting it be another tab.

The plant: four leaves, all holding. I moved it slightly so it gets more of the window and I don't know if that's right but it seemed like the thing to do and it seems okay with it. I've stopped waiting for it to tell me I've done something wrong. Maybe it's just growing.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been telling yourself you'll feel better about once a specific thing happens — and what are you actually doing while you wait?
entries.content:
The thing I keep telling myself is: once the first checkpoint clears, I'll breathe.

Two weeks from Wednesday. I have it memorized in the way you memorize a due date — not because you wrote it down but because your brain tattooed it there without asking. Once that meeting is done and Drew has seen what I've been working on and decided it's acceptable, then I'll be able to actually be in my life again. Then I'll call Nat, actually call her, not just like something in the group chat and tell myself it counts. Then I'll stop eating over my keyboard. Then the apartment will feel like a place I live and not a place I sleep between work and more work.

I've been telling myself this for about eight days.

What I've actually been doing: Sunday I spent four hours on the document, made it worse, spent another hour making it less worse, went to bed at 1am, woke up at 6:45 and looked at the ceiling and thought about the checkpoint. Monday I got through the day and came home and opened the document and closed it and ordered pad thai. Tuesday I had a good stretch in the afternoon, like genuinely productive, three or four solid hours where something was clicking, and I felt this wave of relief and thought *okay, I can do this, I have this* — and then I stopped for dinner and couldn't get back in afterward. The door just closed. I sat at the table with my laptop open until almost midnight trying to push through it and produced maybe one paragraph I don't hate.

Wednesday was yesterday. I didn't open the document at all. Drew stopped by my desk to ask a question about something unrelated and I answered and he nodded and kept walking and I spent approximately the next twenty minutes wondering if the question was actually unrelated or if it was a check-in disguised as a question and Drew was taking a reading on me without telling me that's what was happening. *He would just ask. He's your manager, he would just ask.* Yeah probably. That's probably right. But then again. But then again.

Today is Thursday. The document is in another tab right now. I'm writing this in the notes app on my phone because I couldn't sleep and couldn't work and couldn't just lie here doing nothing, and this felt like the least destructive option.

The *once it's done* thing. I know what it's doing. I know exactly what it's doing. It's making the next two weeks into a hallway I'm just passing through, not a place I actually am. Like nothing between now and the checkpoint is real life, it's just the space before real life starts. But here's the problem with that: if I get through the checkpoint and Drew says something good and the relief comes, I know — I know — I will immediately find the next thing to assign the same weight to. There will be another deliverable, another meeting, another nod I need or didn't get. The hallway will extend itself. It always extends itself. There is no room at the end that I actually get to live in.

I think about the Saturday morning with the coffee. That was four days ago. Four days and it already feels like something I visited.

Avery is asleep. I can hear the breathing from here. I've been lying here next to it for an hour just — existing in the wrong frequency. Avery doesn't do the hallway thing. That's not a knock, it's just true. Avery lives in the actual present day in a way that I watch the way you watch someone do something you can't quite figure out. Like there's a trick to it that I'm missing and if I just kept watching long enough I'd catch it.

*You could wake them up.* No. Not at 1am because I can't stop running laps around a checkpoint meeting. That's not something you do to a person.

But the thing is — and I've been circling this for a while and I'm just going to write it because it's 1am and I'm tired of being careful — Avery is going to ask how things are going. Maybe tomorrow, maybe this weekend. And I'm going to say *fine, it's a lot but I think it's coming together* and Avery is going to hear that and probably believe it and we'll talk about something else. And there will be a moment somewhere in there where I could have said the real thing — *I said yes to something when I should have waited, I did it because I wanted Drew to think I was worth something, and now I'm awake at 1am watching the days pass and calling it waiting* — and I won't say it. I'll come close and then redirect.

I keep thinking about what Ben said. *You seem more settled.* I've been using that as a referendum on something but I don't even know what exactly. Like if the surface is calm enough people will mistake it for the whole picture. Maybe that's the goal I've actually been working toward without realizing it and it's a terrible goal. The checkpoint will clear and I'll seem more settled. Drew will nod and I'll seem more settled. Avery will fall asleep on the couch and I'll sit there in the dark, settled, awake, alone with the version of myself that I haven't figured out how to introduce to anyone.

*This is catastrophizing. You're tired. It's 1am. The document is not that bad.*

The document is not that bad. That's probably true. I have actually done some real work on it. There are parts that are — not Reese-level clean, but honest, which might be a different thing. Things I actually worked out instead of arranging. I keep wanting to believe that counts for something.

The checkpoint will happen and something will follow it. That's all I actually know.

What I'm doing while I wait is living in a city in a small apartment with a plant that keeps adding leaves and a person asleep beside me who poured a second coffee on Saturday morning and didn't say anything about it, just took it, and that means something. It means the kind of thing I forget to account for when I'm doing the math. I'm on a bus every morning that takes twenty-two minutes and the light through the window does something different every day and I keep looking at my phone instead of that. I'm in a kitchen at midnight eating crackers out of the box because I don't want to deal with the dishes, waiting for the next thing that will make it okay to exhale, while the exhale is apparently available right now and I just keep not taking it.

The plant is on the windowsill getting the light I gave it. Four leaves, all staying.

I think I'm going to try to sleep.

entries.prompt: Think about someone you haven't talked to in a while — not because anything happened, just because time slipped. What would you actually say to them if you sat down together tomorrow?
entries.content:
Nat's been in my head all day.

Not in a bad way. She just kept showing up. I was in the elevator this morning and someone was wearing the specific kind of bright orange puffer coat that Nat used to wear in college, the ratty one she refused to throw away, and I just — there she was. Standing in my elevator. Gone when the doors opened. I thought about her on and off all day in that background way, like a tab that's open but not in focus.

It's been two months since we actually talked. Maybe more. The group chat is there, she's in it, she sent a voice memo last week about a date that went sideways and it was twenty-three minutes long and I listened to the whole thing on the bus in two installments and laughed out loud at a specific part and sent back a voice memo that was maybe four minutes because that's the most I had. And that felt like talking. It was not talking.

If I sat down with Nat tomorrow. Okay.

I'd want to tell her about the Saturday morning thing. The coffee, Avery at the window, the pigeon. I know she would get it in a specific way that's different from how anyone else would get it. Nat has this thing where when you describe a moment she asks one question that goes directly underneath the moment, like she can see the thing you're actually trying to say. She'd probably ask something like: *what did you feel when you were standing there?* And I'd have to actually answer it. I couldn't say *fine* or *good, I think* and have that be enough. She'd wait. That's the thing about Nat, she waits. She doesn't fill in the silence the way I do.

I'd tell her I'm scared about the checkpoint, which I haven't said that clearly to anyone. Not to Avery. Not to Ben, when we talked, not even close. I performed some version of *it's a lot but I'm figuring it out* and Ben gave me the settled thing and we moved on. Nat I think I could say: *I raised my hand in a meeting because I wanted my manager to look at me like I was worth something, and now the thing is due in less than two weeks, and I'm awake at weird hours running math about how much Drew thinks of me.* I think she'd hear that and not try to fix it. She'd probably say something a little mean in a fond way, something like *you've been doing that since freshman year* — and she wouldn't be wrong, and somehow that would make it easier to breathe.

I'd tell her I'm worried about us. The group of us. About the thinning. Not in a way that's anyone's fault — that's the thing I want her to understand, it's not accusatory, it's more like something I'm watching happen in slow motion and I don't know what to do about it except notice it and feel bad. I miss Nat. That's the real thing. I miss her in the specific way you miss people who knew you before you got good at managing yourself. She knew the version of me that didn't spend eight minutes writing two sentences. She knew the version that just said things.

*What's Avery like* — she'd ask that, and I'd want to get it right. I'd say: patient. Settled in this way that's different from how I'm settled, which is the wrong word for me anyway, I'm not settled, I'm maintaining shape. Avery is actually settled. Avery makes coffee and hands it to you and doesn't say anything about it and that's the whole thing. I'd say I'm scared about it sometimes in ways I can't fully explain, not because of anything Avery's done but because I keep waiting to be found insufficient. I'd say I don't tell Avery enough of the real stuff and I know it and I can see myself not doing it while I'm doing it. Nat would probably say *why* and I'd probably shrug and she'd probably not accept the shrug. That's the thing about actually sitting down with someone. You can't send a four-minute voice memo and call it enough.

We'd end up talking about something stupid eventually. We always did — the real thing would happen and then we'd run out of real thing and slide sideways into something completely different, some memory or some bit about a shared thing from college, and it would be easy and dumb and good. I miss that too. The slide. The way real conversations have an out, a place where the weight lifts a little and you're just two people laughing about something that doesn't mean anything.

Here's what I'd actually want to say to her though, if I could say one thing without preamble, without working up to it:

*I think I'm okay and I'm not totally sure okay is what I mean.*

Which is not a sentence that fits in a voice memo and not something you can bury at the end of a group chat week full of links and screenshots. It requires sitting down. It requires someone who will hear it and not immediately move to tell you what okay really means or list the reasons you should feel it more. It requires Nat specifically, actually, because she would hear the whole thing — the okay and the not sure — and hold them both at the same time without picking one.

I'm not going to see Nat tomorrow. She's three time zones and a $400 flight away and we both have jobs and lives that don't flex easily. But the checkpoint is in twelve days and after the checkpoint maybe there's a weekend. Maybe I actually suggest the trip. Not in the group chat, not just throwing the idea into the pile where it dissolves. Maybe I call Nat. Then Priya, then Ben. Maybe I do the thing that the me from college would have done, the one who sent the first text, who booked the thing.

I keep meaning to be that person and not doing it. But I also called Ben. Six weeks of not calling and then I called. The plant was dying and then I moved it and now there are four leaves.

Things are capable of not going the way they've been going. I know this. I just keep acting like I don't.

It's late. The checkpoint document is in another tab and I've been ignoring it since dinner and tomorrow I need to actually open it and not make it worse. But tonight I just wanted to write this down. The elevator. The orange coat. The question of what I'd say if I had the chance to just sit down and say it.

Nat, if somehow you ever read this: I think you're one of my actual favorite people and I've been coasting on you and I'm sorry about that and I miss the slide. I miss the part after the real part.

Okay. Four leaves. Twelve days. Sleep.

entries.prompt: What's something you've said recently that you immediately wanted to take back — not because it was wrong, but because saying it out loud made it more real than you were ready for?
entries.content:
The thing I said was to Avery. Three days ago, Tuesday, in the kitchen after dinner.

We'd been doing the dishes together — Avery washes, I dry, this thing we settled into without deciding to — and it was quiet in the good way, not the loaded way, just two people getting through a thing. And Avery said something about the fall, some mention of a friend's wedding upstate in October, asked if I'd want to come if it worked out. Casual. Not a big question. And I said sure, obviously, yeah.

And then Avery said: *it'd be nice. I want to show you off.*

Which was — fine, that's a warm thing to say, I know that. And instead of just taking it I said, the words coming out before I'd cleared them: *sometimes I wonder if you'll get tired of me first.*

Out loud. In the kitchen. With a damp dish towel in my hand.

Avery went quiet for a second. Not a bad quiet, not hurt exactly — more like the quiet of someone putting something down carefully. Then: *what does that mean?*

And I tried to walk it back immediately, said it came out wrong, said something about work stress and being tired, did the whole redirect. Avery let me do it, didn't push, and we finished the dishes and moved to the couch and watched something and it was fine. Avery brought it up gently once more before we fell asleep — *you know you can say things to me* — and I said I know, and meant it, and still didn't say anything.

I have been thinking about those seven words for three days.

*Sometimes I wonder if you'll get tired of me first.*

The *first* is the part. That's the part I didn't mean to include. Tired of me, fine, that's almost a generic relationship anxiety, almost something you could make a joke out of. But *first* means I'll get there eventually. *First* means I'm already tracking the clock and calculating who will exhaust whom and I've already assigned myself the role of the one who will wear the other out. *First* means I've been thinking about the ending.

And I have not been consciously thinking about the ending. I want to be clear about that, or clear to myself at least. Things with Avery are — they're good. Saturday with the coffee. The sleeping on the couch. The way Avery handed me back the movie remote without a word when I wanted to rewatch something and said it was terrible the first time and still sat through the whole thing. The wanting to show me off at a wedding in October. I have all of this. I have been writing about it in these entries as evidence of something real and I believe that evidence. I'm not looking for the exit.

But the word came out and it was apparently in there, loaded, waiting. Seven words that had been sitting somewhere in my chest being very quiet about the fact that they existed.

*Why did you say it out loud. Of all the times to lose the filter.* 

I know why. We were doing something domestic and easy and Avery said a kind thing and some part of me that doesn't trust kind things got scared and put it outside the body before I could review it. Like trying to get rid of it. Like maybe if it was out in the kitchen it wouldn't be in my chest anymore. Classic.

It's still in my chest.

The not-pushing-back is almost the harder part. If Avery had said *what do you mean, no, that's not how this works, here's why you're wrong* — I'd have something to argue with or be reassured by or push off of. Instead I got the careful quiet and then *you can say things to me.* Which is more generous than I deserved and also puts the whole thing back in my hands. Avery can't fix a thing I won't name. I know this. I have been writing around this for months. I get close to the real version and then hear the alarm. The alarm has been getting louder.

Reese asked me today, in the most offhand way, how the workstream thing was going. Nine days to the checkpoint. I said fine, coming together. Same voice I use when Avery asks about work. The managed voice. Reese nodded and we moved on and I sat back down at my desk and thought: I said something real in the kitchen three days ago and my first instinct was to apologize for it.

I don't know what to do with that as a fact about myself. It's not new information. But I've been carrying it harder this week.

There is a thing I want and I don't have the exact words for it yet. It's something like: I want to stop treating the real stuff as a hazmat situation. I want to be able to say *sometimes I think you'll get tired of me* and have it be a thing I said, not a thing I detonated. I want the alarm to go quiet long enough for me to finish a sentence. I'm not there. I'm clearly not there.

Ben would probably say I've always been like this and mean it kindly. Nat would ask the question that goes underneath. Avery is three feet away most nights and has told me directly, twice now, *you can say things to me* — which is about as clear an invitation as a person can extend — and I am standing outside it like there's a velvet rope.

Tonight I'm on the couch and Avery is making tea in the kitchen and I can hear the kettle. The checkpoint is in nine days and after the checkpoint maybe I breathe. Maybe I stop running the math for one night. Maybe I say something that I haven't drafted eight versions of first.

*Sometimes I wonder if you'll get tired of me first.*

I think what it actually means — what I actually meant, what's been sitting in the chest — is: I don't totally understand why you're still being patient. I'm a lot. I'm awake at 1am doing laps. I'm the guy with the alarm, the managed Slack messages, the yes in the conference room, the damp dish towel and the accidental real sentence. I don't understand what you see from where you're standing and it scares me that I might never fully understand it, and also it scares me that understanding it would mean I'd have to believe it, and believing it would mean I couldn't keep holding the ending at arm's length like some kind of hedge.

The kettle just clicked off. Avery's in there.

I have a plant with four leaves that was dying when I started paying attention to it. 

I think I need to just — say a true thing. Not the whole essay. Just one true thing, without the redirect. Tonight. While the tea is still hot.

entries.prompt: What's something you did this week that you'd describe differently depending on who was asking — the version you'd tell a close friend versus the version you'd tell yourself at midnight?
entries.content:
The work version first, since that's the one I've been practicing.

I had a strong week on the workstream. Made real progress, incorporated some useful feedback from a touchpoint with Drew mid-week, feeling good going into the checkpoint. That's what I'd tell someone who asked casually. That's the version I ran through in my head this morning like a line I was going to need.

Drew stopped by my desk Wednesday — not the checkpoint, just a check-in, informal — and asked how I was tracking. I said: good, I think the structure's coming together, I've been reworking the middle section and I think it's stronger. Drew nodded, said sounds right, keep going. Three sentences. Maybe thirty seconds. And I sat there after he walked away and felt the specific high of the nod — the one I said yes in the conference room for, the one I've been chasing — and let myself have it for about two minutes before the voice arrived to tell me the nod meant nothing yet, the nod was not the checkpoint, the nod was just Drew being a person who nods while walking away.

But I'd tell Nat: I got the nod and I needed it so badly I almost texted her right after. I didn't. I opened the document and made it better while the nod was still warm. That's the true version. The needing it and then turning it into something instead of just sitting in it. I don't know if that's healthy or not. It got two paragraphs done that I actually don't hate, so.

Here's the thing I did that I'd tell a different story about depending on who was asking.

Tuesday night. Avery made tea. I was on the couch. The kettle clicked off. I wrote last time that I was going to say a true thing while the tea was still hot and I want to be honest about what happened, which is: mostly I did it. Not perfectly. Not the whole essay I had written in my head for two months. But Avery came in with the two mugs and sat down and I said — I didn't draft it, I just let it happen — *I've been weird lately. I know I've been weird. I think I'm scared of the checkpoint and I'm taking it sideways instead of saying that.*

Avery was quiet for a second and then said: I know.

And I said: I also think I meant the thing I said in the kitchen. About you getting tired. I don't think it's going to happen. But it's in there.

And Avery put down the tea and looked at me and said: *I'm not going anywhere. I need you to know that I'm not somewhere keeping score.*

And I almost redirected. I felt the alarm, the reflexive move toward *no yeah I know, it's fine, I'm fine* — I felt all of that arrive and I sat on it and said: okay. I'm trying to believe that.

And that was it. We drank the tea. We didn't solve anything. Nothing got resolved in a meaningful way except that the sentence got said and Avery heard it and didn't leave and the apartment didn't catch fire. Which sounds like the lowest possible bar but has been the bar in my head for months apparently.

What I'd tell a close friend: I did the thing. Kind of. Partial credit. It was terrifying and also completely fine and now I have to figure out what to do with the information that it was completely fine.

What I'd tell myself at midnight: you got halfway. You said the work thing and the scared-of-losing-Avery thing but you didn't say the underneath part. The part about not understanding what Avery sees. The part about how I've been walking around with the ending held at arm's length like it's a thing I need to protect against instead of just a fear. You said the surface layer of the real thing and that was good but there's another floor.

There's always another floor.

I told Nat about the checkpoint yesterday. Called her, actually called her, didn't text first to ask if she was free, just called. She picked up on the third ring and said *oh my god an actual phone call* in this voice that was teasing but warm, and I laughed, and then we talked for almost forty minutes. I told her the real version — the conference room, the yes, the nod I needed, the nights at 1am doing laps. She said, exactly as I predicted, *you've been doing that since freshman year* and I said yeah I know and she said *that doesn't make it fine, I just want you to know I see it.* And there was the question that goes underneath: *what happens if the checkpoint goes well? What do you do with the energy you've been putting into dreading it?*

I didn't have an answer for that. I said probably I'd find something else to assign it to and she said probably and we both knew that was the honest answer and it was enough for someone to know.

The version I'd tell anyone else: touched base with an old friend, it was good to catch up.

The version at midnight: I've been treating that friendship like something that would be there when I got around to it and I called her four days before a checkpoint I'm terrified of because I needed someone who knew the version of me that existed before I got good at performing being fine, and she answered, and it was easy, and I've been an idiot about this for months.

Six days to the checkpoint. I've stopped making it worse. That's real progress in the only way that matters which is the actual document is now in a state where I could turn it in and not be embarrassed. Whether it's what Drew is looking for I won't know until Wednesday and there's no amount of midnight lapping that will change that now.

Avery brought me coffee this morning without being asked. Put it next to the laptop and went back to making eggs. Didn't say anything about it.

I sat there and looked at the mug and thought: this is evidence. You have been collecting evidence and refusing to file it anywhere and maybe that's the thing to actually work on. Not the checkpoint. Not the nod. Not whether Reese's structures are tidier than mine. Just — learning to file the evidence where it belongs. To let the coffee mean what the coffee means.

Five leaves now. The plant just added one, out of nowhere, no warning. Small and pale green and coming in from the same side as the last one, like it found a direction that works.

Wednesday. Then I find out.

entries.prompt: What's something you're better at performing than feeling — and when did you last catch yourself doing it?
entries.content:
Confidence. That's the answer before I even finish reading the question.

I perform confidence the way some people perform busyness — constantly, without thinking about it, as a first line of defense before anything else. The voice that says *yeah, I've got room for that.* The Slack message that took eight minutes to look like it took thirty seconds. The Drew check-in where I said *I think the structure's coming together* in a tone that implied I'd never doubted it, when what was actually happening was I'd been awake at 1am three nights in a row rearranging the same section and each time being less sure I'd improved it.

I caught it this morning. That's when. I want to be specific because I've been vague about this stuff for long enough.

Reese came by my desk around 9:30, just to look at something briefly, some shared file situation, nothing to do with the workstream. And we were talking for maybe four minutes and Reese said, offhand, *you seem really calm about Wednesday.* Meaning the checkpoint. And I said — immediately, no lag, fully formed — *yeah, I think I'm in a decent place with it.*

Smile included. Eye contact. The whole thing.

Reese nodded and went back to their desk.

And I sat there and thought: that was so fast. I didn't even feel myself do it. Some part of me heard *you seem calm* and treated it like an instruction. Like the sentence was asking me to confirm, and I confirmed, reflexively, before I could take a breath and ask whether it was actually true.

It's not actually true. Or it's not — complete. I'm not full-out panicking. The document is okay, I said that last time and I meant it, there are parts of it I'm genuinely okay with. But calm implies something settled, something I've made peace with, and what's happening in my body is closer to a refrigerator hum I've just gotten used to. The checkpoint is in three days and I can feel it in my shoulders. That's not calm. That's adaptation.

The thing about performing confidence specifically is that it doubles back on you in this gross way. Because now Reese thinks I'm calm, and that means I have to maintain the calm version or it'll look like something changed, and nothing changed except Reese found out. So I've created a new piece of staging to manage. A sub-performance inside the main one. Great. Efficient system.

I keep wondering when I started doing this. Like the origin, the specific point. I have some memory of being younger and just — saying things as they were. Scared before a test, saying I'm scared before a test. Nat knew the non-performed version. Ben did. There's a photograph from sophomore year where I'm mid-laugh at something, and I look at that sometimes and think: that person was not reviewing their laugh before sending it. That person was just laughing.

I don't know what happened. Somewhere between then and the conference room I learned to prep the version and lead with that. Probably not one thing. Probably just the accumulation of moments where the performed version went over better than whatever was underneath would have.

But it's exhausting. That's the thing I notice most. Not even in a philosophical way, just in an actual body way. It is tiring to load the right answer before the question is done. By 6pm I've been doing it so long that the *whatever* comes out when Avery calls because I don't have any more managed left.

The Tuesday night thing with Avery — the tea, the two sentences I actually said — I've been turning that over all week. Because that was the opposite of performing. That was just the thing, unformatted, arriving in the kitchen. And nothing happened. Avery didn't go anywhere. The apartment didn't catch fire. I know I said that before but I'm still processing it, I think. Still waiting for the data to integrate. *The unperformed version was okay.* I have that written down now in the actual evidence. And still, this morning, Reese said I seemed calm and I performed calm back at them before the thought arrived.

It's not a switch. I know that. But I notice the gap between what I'm apparently capable of at 9:30pm with tea and what happens automatically at 9:30am under fluorescent light.

The checkpoint is Wednesday. Two more days of the refrigerator hum.

I thought about this on the bus home. Not the checkpoint — well, the checkpoint, but also the larger version of the question. What do I actually feel when I'm not monitoring what I feel. This week it's been: scared about Wednesday. Weirdly hopeful about the Nat call, like that door is back open a little. Grateful for Avery in a way I keep feeling and keep not delivering to the person it's about. Tired. Genuinely, in my bones tired, not just sleep-tired but the tired that comes from running two tracks at once for a long time.

Those are the things I feel. Most of them I haven't said out loud, except scattered parts to scattered people at scattered moments. The confident version, the calm version, the *coming together* version — that one gets said out loud. All day. To whoever's asking.

I want Wednesday to be over. That's the most honest thing. I want to be standing on the other side of it, whatever that side looks like, because at least then the anticipating is done and I'm dealing with the real thing instead of the imagined version I've been living in for three weeks. Even if it goes badly — even if Drew has questions I can't answer or the structure is wrong or something I missed turns out to matter — at least it'll be information. Right now it's just static.

I texted Avery on the bus. Just: *can we get food together tonight, I don't want to eat over my keyboard again.* And Avery said: *yes, obviously, the Thai place?* And I said yeah. 

Not a big thing. Just wanted to say something real before I got home, something that was just — me wanting the thing and asking for it. No management required.

The plant has five leaves and I stopped waiting for the catch. I don't know when that happened but somewhere in the last week I stopped walking past it expecting to find something wrong. I just water it. I moved it toward the light. It does what it does.

Wednesday. Then I find out what the static was actually hiding.

entries.prompt: When's the last time you felt genuinely proud of yourself — not relieved that something went well, but actually proud? Walk me through the moment: what happened, and why did it land differently?
entries.content:
Wednesday happened.

Drew had the document up on his screen when I came in — I could see it before I sat down, my name in the header, the version from Monday — and he was already in this mode where he's reading while he's also vaguely tracking you. I sat in the chair across from him, the one that's slightly lower than his chair, whether that's intentional or just how the room got set up I still don't know. I had a coffee. My hand wasn't shaking, which I'm mentioning because it was a near thing on the elevator up.

He asked me to walk him through the middle section. The one I'd been rearranging at 1am three weeks in a row. The one I'd made worse and then less worse and then finally, about five days ago, made into something I could stand behind.

I walked him through it.

He asked two questions. One I'd anticipated, one I hadn't. The one I hadn't anticipated, I thought for a second — an actual second, not a performed thinking pause but a real one — and said: *I don't know, but here's what I'd want to figure out before we move forward.* And then I said what I'd want to figure out. And Drew nodded, not the reflexive walking-away nod, a different one. Slower. Like he was actually receiving something.

At the end he said: *this is solid work. You got there.*

*You got there.* Four words. Not *great job* or *looks good* or any of the things that function as punctuation at the end of a meeting. *You got there* — which implies he'd been watching me not be there, which should maybe bother me, but the way he said it wasn't like that. It was like watching someone make it across something difficult and saying: yeah, I saw the whole thing, I saw you work.

I walked back to my desk and sat down and didn't do anything for probably three minutes. Just sat there.

That's the moment. That's the one that felt different.

Here's what I'm trying to work out: it wasn't relief. That's the thing. I've felt relief before — the checkpoint-is-over relief, the Drew-walked-away-nodding relief, the relief that's just the absence of dread. This wasn't that. It was something that took up more space. Something that didn't depend on what Drew was going to say next or whether Reese had done it cleaner or whether the structure was the right one according to some external measure I can't see. It was something about — I know what I did. I know the nights I put into it. I know the part where it was genuinely bad and I kept going anyway and I know the part where I stopped making it worse and started making it better and I know why the middle section is the way it is, not because it's tidy but because I worked something out in it instead of just arranging it to look right.

*You got there.* I got there. That's mine. Nobody handed it to me.

Why did it land differently. I think because I wasn't performing my way through it. The one question I didn't anticipate — that's the test, right, that's the thing you can't prep the version for, the thing that catches you in real time — and I didn't reach for the confident voice or the blank nod. I said I don't know and then said something true about what I'd do next. That's not a thing I would have done three weeks ago. Three weeks ago I would have tried to answer the question I didn't have an answer for, would have manufactured something plausible, would have spent the bus ride home wondering if Drew had noticed.

I noticed that I didn't do that. That's maybe the part I'm actually proud of. Not just the document. The moment where I was in the room and I was actually in the room, not in a parallel room managing what the room could see.

I texted Nat from the bathroom afterward. Not a voice memo, just a text: *checkpoint done. I think it went well. You asked what I'd do with the energy afterward and I don't know yet but I'm standing here finding out.* She sent back three words and a string of something and the three words were *told you, dummy.* Which is exactly correct and exactly the right thing to say.

I told Avery tonight. The actual version. The two questions, the one I couldn't answer, the *I don't know but here's what I'd want to figure out.* I told Avery that was the thing, that that was the moment, and Avery listened and then said: *you're more interesting when you stop trying to seem like you have it together.* Said it like it was obvious. Said it like it was fond.

I didn't deflect. I just said: yeah. I'm starting to think that's right.

And I meant it. I didn't hear the alarm. It just — sat there and was true and I let it.

I don't want to make this too neat. The fridge hum is not entirely gone. I'm already vaguely aware of the next deliverable, the way it's appearing on the edge of things, the way I can feel the pull to make that the new checkpoint, the new thing standing between me and real life. I know that about myself now, I know the pattern, and knowing it doesn't fully break it. But I caught myself doing it on the bus home and I thought: no. Not tonight. Tonight you found out what the static was hiding and it was something okay. Let it be okay for tonight.

The apartment felt different when I came in. Or I felt different in it, which is probably the same thing. I watered the plant, which has five leaves and is apparently just going to keep doing that, just going to keep adding leaves without asking my permission or waiting for conditions to be right. I made tea without Avery asking. I stood in the kitchen and didn't think about the next thing for almost ten full minutes and it was — quiet. Just quiet.

*You got there.* I keep saying it back to myself not to inflate it but because it's true in a way that doesn't require anything else to be true first. The document was solid. The middle section held. I said *I don't know* in a room with my manager and the room didn't end.

I got there. That's mine to keep.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been looking forward to lately that, when you actually imagine it arriving, doesn't feel the way you thought it would? Walk me through what you expected — and what's actually there when you picture it now.
entries.content:
The trip.

The trip has been the answer to every low-level question for about a year now. *When are you going to actually see your people?* The trip. *Why haven't you called Nat in two months?* The trip is coming. *Doesn't it bother you that the group chat is mostly links?* The trip will fix it, the trip will be the thing where we're all back in the same room and the thinning stops and we remember who we are to each other and everything snaps back into whatever it was before distance and jobs and becoming people separately.

I brought it up on the Nat call last week. Post-checkpoint, standing in the bathroom, energy I didn't have a container for yet. And she said yes immediately — *yes, let's actually do it this time, you plan it, I'll show up wherever* — and I felt that sharp alive thing in my chest, that old momentum, the version of me that booked the thing. I got off the call and opened four tabs. Started looking at dates and places and what a long weekend would cost. Sent a message to the group chat that was more than a link, an actual proposal: first weekend of November, somewhere in the middle geographically, I'll find a place if you all say you're in.

Ben said yes within an hour. Priya sent a voice memo that was nine minutes long and was mostly yes plus a story about a coworker I've never met plus yes again. Nat texted me separately: *you're doing it, I'm proud of you, find somewhere with a porch.*

So it's happening. Probably. Everyone said yes and I've been looking at houses in the mountains two states over and there's one that sleeps six with a porch and a fireplace and costs a reasonable amount split four ways and I should just book it.

I haven't booked it yet.

And I'm trying to be honest about why.

When I picture actually getting there — driving or taking a bus or whatever, arriving somewhere with a porch, everyone pulling bags out of cars at slightly different times the way it always goes — when I actually hold that image in my head and let it run, something happens that's not the feeling I expected. I expected the sharp alive thing. I expected the version of the feeling I had when Nat said yes.

What's actually there when I picture it is something quieter and more complicated. Something a little like anticipatory grief, which is a phrase I've never used in my life but seems accurate.

Because these are not the same people. That's the thing I almost wrote to Ben before the February call and then didn't. We've all been becoming people separately. Nat has a whole life in her city with friends I've never met and a job that changed her in ways I catch glimpses of on the phone. Ben is going through something with his apartment, with his direction, that I got the surface of when we talked and not the inside. Priya sends voice memos instead of texts because she's someone who processes out loud now and I don't know when that started. And I'm — whatever I am. The checkpoint person. The plant person. The guy who spent eight months writing managed Slack messages and then one Tuesday night said a real thing over tea.

We've been accumulating lives and the trip is going to be the first time we have to be in the same room with each other and all of those lives at once.

I think what I expected was that the trip would feel like going back. Like finding the original file, uncorrupted. All of us around a table, the shorthand returning, the easy stupid thing after the real thing the way Nat and I always end up. *That* — I've been counting on that, storing it up, keeping it as the reward for getting through all this.

What I actually think it will be is something harder and better than going back. Something where we have to introduce each other to who we've become. Where the shorthand has gaps in it now, places where a reference lands and only half the room remembers why it's funny. Where someone says *you've changed* and means it as a good thing and it still sits strangely. Where we have to work a little, the way you work to relearn a thing your hands forgot.

That's not nothing. That's actually — I think that's worth doing. I think that's the thing underneath the thing, the reason the trip kept not happening. Not logistics. Not money. The fact that once we do it we'll know. We'll know what the gap actually is, whether it's small or bigger than we think, and we'll have to figure out who we are to each other from the people we currently are instead of who we were. And I've been wanting to preserve the uncertainty, the possibility that we're all fine, that the group chat is just one form of the thing and the thing itself is intact.

Ben said something, on the call in February — *you seem more settled than when we last talked* — and it bothered me for months, I kept picking it up and putting it down. I think I've finally figured out why. Because it meant he was looking at me from a distance and reading me as a different person than I felt like from the inside. And I'd been doing the same thing to them. Carrying around the 2021 version, the college version, the version that exists in the group chat and the voice memos and the shorthand. The one that isn't complicated by having become someone without me watching.

The porch house has a fireplace. First weekend of November will probably be cold enough to use it. I can picture us there, four people in coats on a porch with drinks, and it won't be the same as being 21 in someone's bad apartment and it will be better and it will also be a thing I have to grieve a little to receive.

I should book it. I'm going to book it.

Not because it'll feel the way I thought it would — it won't, I know that now, the image in my head is already more complicated than the one I started with — but because complicated-and-real is better than preserved-and-theoretical. I kept the friendship in my chest and didn't call Ben for six weeks. I kept Avery at arm's length and ate takeout over my keyboard and called it fine. I'm tired of keeping things somewhere safe where I can't actually have them.

November. The porch. Find out what the gap actually is.

I'm opening the tab now. I'm going to put in the dates and the card number and close it before I can talk myself out of it.

The plant has six leaves. I counted tonight. The sixth came in while I wasn't looking, which is apparently how it works — you're not watching and then it's just there, a thing that happened while you were busy waiting for something else to matter.

Six leaves. Book the house. Find out.

entries.prompt: What's something you've let yourself want recently — really want, not just "it would be nice if" — and what happened in the days after you admitted it, even just to yourself?
entries.content:
I booked the house.

Friday night, eleven-something, laptop open at the kitchen table while Avery was in the other room. Put in the dates, the card number, hit confirm before whatever part of me was going to stop it could arrive. And then I just — sat there. Looking at the confirmation email. A photo of a porch with two wooden chairs and a mountain in the background that's probably prettier in the photo than in person and I don't care, I don't care, it's real now.

I sent the screenshot to the group chat at 11:23 and went to bed.

That's not the thing I wanted. The trip is real now and I'm glad and that's not the thing.

Here's the thing.

I've been sitting with this for about five days and I haven't written it down yet because writing it down means I have to see it flat and decide if it's what I think it is. So here it is flat.

I want to stay.

Not in this apartment necessarily, not this specific configuration. But — this. The coffee on Saturday mornings. The way Avery says the names of things, like with opinions, like a cup of tea is a *good cup of tea* or a bad one, not just a cup of tea. The breathing from the other side of the bed when I can't sleep. The fact that when I said *I don't understand why you're still being patient* — or the version of that I managed to actually say — Avery just said *I'm not going anywhere* and meant it plainly, without ceremony, the way you'd say *it's raining* or *the milk's almost out.* Not a promise. Just a fact.

I want this to be the thing. For a long time.

That's what I admitted to myself sometime last week. I don't know exactly when. Maybe on the bus. Maybe standing at the window in the gray-blue morning. At some point the sentence just arrived and I looked at it and recognized it was true and then waited for the alarm and the alarm didn't come, or came quieter, or came and I was just too tired to jump.

I want this.

The days after have been — strange. Not bad. Just strange in the way anything is strange when you've been holding it at a managed distance and then put it down and look at it in its actual size.

The size is large. That's the thing. It's larger than *it would be nice if.* Larger than *things are good right now.* It has weight to it and direction and I can feel it when Avery is around in a way that's different from before, not better or worse, just — I know what the feeling is now and knowing makes it feel like I'm carrying something in my hands instead of in my pocket where I couldn't see it.

Monday Avery made dinner, actual dinner, not takeout, something with vegetables and actual effort, and I was sitting on the counter watching and not helping enough and Avery threw a dish towel at me and said *you could at least stir this* and I stirred it and we ate at the actual table and I kept thinking: *you want this.* Like a fact I had to keep re-verifying.

Tuesday I almost said it. We were on the couch after dinner and Avery was reading something on a phone and I was not reading anything, just there, just in the room, and I thought: *say the thing.* Not the whole thing, not an announcement, just — something that lets it be real outside my own head. And I got as far as *hey* and Avery looked up and I said: *I'm glad the checkpoint is done.* Which is true and completely not the thing.

Avery said *I know, you've been more here this week* and went back to reading.

*More here.* Avery noticed. That's — something.

I've been trying to figure out what's stopping me from saying it fully and I think it's this: saying *I want this to be the thing, for a long time* means wanting something out loud that I could still lose. The kept-in-pocket version is safer. If I don't say it and it ends, I can tell myself I always knew it might. If I say it — if it becomes a real thing I said to a real person while they were reading their phone on my couch — then I've committed to the hope. And committing to the hope means the losing would be the real size of what I wanted and not just the managed version.

*That's the whole thing, isn't it. You've been writing around this for months.*

Yeah. I know.

And here's the part where it gets more complicated, which is that I also know — I wrote it in an earlier entry and it was true then and it's still true — that Avery is not somewhere keeping score. Avery made dinner on a Monday. Avery poured a second coffee and didn't say anything about it. Avery said *I'm not going anywhere* like a statement of fact and then let me process it at my own absurd pace without making me feel absurd.
