The evidence is there. I've been collecting it. I just haven't fully filed it.

I think I'm going to tell Avery about the November trip this weekend — really tell her, not just show the confirmation email, but say: *this is something I've been scared to do and I did it.* And maybe that's the door. Maybe I say that and then the other thing is right there and I just let it keep going.

Or maybe I overthink it and it comes out sideways and Avery looks at me the way Avery does when I've made something complicated that didn't need to be, that slightly fond exasperated look, and I start laughing at myself and say: *I've been trying to tell you I want this. For a long time. I'm slow at saying things.*

And maybe that's fine. Maybe that's actually fine.

The plant has six leaves and a seventh coming in. I can see it at the edge of the stem, small and pale, just starting. I didn't notice it earlier in the week and now I can't stop noticing it. 

November has a porch. I filed the evidence. The thing I want has a shape now and I know what it is.

*You can still have it.*

That's the sentence I keep saying back to myself when the alarm starts up. A little uncertain still, a little rough around the pronunciation. But I'm saying it. That's new. That's the thing that's different from a month ago, or three months ago, or whenever this started.

You can still have it. You haven't done anything that makes it impossible. Say the thing. Put it down in front of a person who has, by every available evidence, been waiting for you to do exactly that.

Seven leaves soon.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been meaning to say to someone — not because there's a problem, but because you've been carrying it around and it would mean something to them to hear it? Walk me through what's stopping you.
entries.content:
Last night I finally said it.

Not all of it. But the real part.

We were in bed, not late exactly, maybe 10:30, the kind of evening where nothing specific happened but the day had been long in a low-level way. Avery was almost asleep — I could tell by the breathing, the particular slowdown I've learned — and I was staring at the ceiling doing the thing where I'm not thinking about anything on purpose and end up thinking about everything. And the sentence was just there. The same one it's been for a week, sitting in my chest in a way that was starting to feel less like anticipation and more like pressure.

*I'm glad you're here. I mean — not just right now. Generally. In my life.*

I said it to the ceiling first, almost no volume, like I was testing it.

And Avery, who I was pretty sure was asleep, said: *I know.*

And then, quieter: *I'm glad I'm here too.*

That was it. The room went quiet again and I lay there feeling something I don't totally have a word for, something like the moment after you put down something heavy and your arms are still adjusting to the absence of it. Relief isn't right. Relief is the checkpoint, relief is the nod, relief is the absence of the bad thing. This was more like: arrival. Something finally in the right place.

I fell asleep fast, which almost never happens.

But I want to write about what was stopping me, because that's the thing, that's the part I've been circling. Because the sentence isn't complicated. *I'm glad you're here, generally, in my life.* There's nothing in there that requires anything back. I wasn't asking a question, wasn't opening a negotiation, wasn't putting anything at risk. It was just a true thing in the direction of a person who has been doing everything possible to make it easy for me to say true things.

So what was stopping me.

I've been thinking about this since the alarm went off this morning. On the bus. In the bad overhead light of the work kitchen where I was standing with a bad cup of coffee and thinking instead of drinking it.

Here's the answer I keep arriving at, which is uncomfortable to write flat: I think I didn't want Avery to know that I needed to say it. Saying *I'm glad you're here* gives away that I'd been uncertain about whether I was allowed to be glad. It's not just warmth. It's also a confession. It says: I have been walking around with this, unsure whether to trust it, and I've decided to trust it, and here's the evidence of the whole anxious process. It lets someone see that you did work to get somewhere that maybe should have been obvious. That you had to convince yourself of something that the other person probably assumed was settled.

*You spent months on this.* Yeah. I know. And saying the thing means admitting that.

There's a version of me I've been trying to protect — the version that handles things, that seems settled, that doesn't make the people he loves do the maintenance on his ability to accept a good thing. I didn't want Avery to know how many laps I'd done around a sentence that simple. I wanted to just feel it cleanly and effortlessly, the way I imagine other people feel things. Not arrive at it out of breath.

But here's what Avery actually heard. Not the laps. Not the months of it. Just: *I'm glad you're here, generally, in my life.* And said *I know* and *I'm glad too.* And went back to sleep. No ceremony. No weight assigned to how long it took me to say it. Just two people in the dark saying a true thing at low volume.

I've been making the gap wider by imagining it wider. That's the thing I keep understanding in new layers. The gap between the managed version and the real version isn't as visible to anyone else as it is to me. I walk around feeling like I'm handing people a performance and they'll notice the seams, and mostly they're just — receiving what's there and responding to it. Avery didn't hear *I'm glad you're here* and think *finally, it only took him eight months.* Avery just heard the true thing.

That's almost harder to sit with than the laps would be. Because it means most of the work, most of the agonizing and redirecting and alarm-hearing, is mine. Something I do privately and then clean up by the time it gets to the other person. Which is either a useful filter or a very tiring way to live and possibly both.

I texted Nat this morning. Nothing long. Just: *I said the thing. Or a version of it. Ceiling, 10:30pm, she was mostly asleep.* She sent back a voice memo — thirty-two seconds, which is short for Nat — that was basically just her saying *of course she already knew, you idiot, she's been waiting, everyone could see this but you* in a voice that was entirely fond. Then at the end, right before she hung up: *I'm proud of you. November's going to be good.*

November. Porch. Fireplace. Find out what the gap is.

I want to say something to Ben too. Different thing. Not the Avery thing — that's its own category — but something I've been carrying around since the February call. When he said *you seem more settled* I've been treating it as a referendum, holding it up against myself every few weeks and finding it slightly sad. But the truth is he said it warmly. He said it like he was glad. And I've never said back: *hey, I noticed you were going through something that night, the apartment stuff, and I didn't ask enough about it, I gave you the surface version of my stuff and didn't push enough on yours.* I've been thinking about that since entry one, basically. The call where I decided we needed to talk more and then immediately did the same thing we always do. He opened a door, a real one, and I knocked and waved through the window and left.

I'm not going to text him that. That's a call. That's another actual call, not from the bus, probably from the block at night like the February one was, walking around the apartment unable to get comfortable.

I'll do it this week. Not a plan in the pile. A call. Something real.

The thing about finally saying things: it doesn't close the feeling, it just changes the shape of it. I'm not done wanting to stay. I'm not done being scared I'll be too much or not enough or that Avery will at some point do the math and find me lacking. I'm not done running laps. But something shifted last night, something small and real, the way a window opens and the whole room is slightly different even when nothing in the room changed.

*I'm glad you're here.* Said it. She heard it. The ceiling witnessed.

Seven leaves on the plant. The seventh is fully in now, not pale anymore, just green and there. Doesn't need me to notice it to be real. Just grew.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been doing lately mostly out of habit — a routine, a ritual, a small regular thing — that you've started to feel differently about? Walk me through the last time you did it.
entries.content:
The bus in the morning. Twenty-two minutes, same route, same seat if I can get it — window side, about two-thirds back, right where the heating vent does something marginally useful in the cold. I've been doing it every weekday for almost two years and for most of that time it's been — nothing. A gap between places. Headphones in, phone out, look up when I think I'm close to the stop.

This week something shifted and I can't fully account for it.

Tuesday. I got on at my usual stop, got the seat, put the headphones in — and then just didn't press play. I don't know why. Sometimes you pick up your phone and then forget what you were going to do with it and by the time you remember the moment has become something else. I was looking out the window and it was one of those November mornings where the sky hasn't decided yet, that color that's either going to be overcast all day or break open around ten. Buildings. Someone waiting to cross. A coffee cart with a short line and steam coming off the cups.

I sat there for the whole twenty-two minutes. Just looking.

And here's what I noticed, which I've apparently been riding past every morning without seeing: there are regulars. I don't mean I recognized faces exactly, more that there are textures. The man with the very specific briefcase that is either vintage or expensive, I can never tell. The two women who always get on together at the 14th Street stop and stand even when there are seats and talk without pausing for the entirety of their ride. A teenager who gets on three stops after me and reads an actual paperback, not a phone, a book with a broken spine that they hold with one hand and brace against the seat in front. I don't know any of them. I've been riding the same bus with some of them for a year and a half and the headphones made them scenery and the no-headphones Tuesday made them people who have a morning, just like I have a morning, all of us pointed somewhere with something waiting at the end of it.

*Why are you being weird about a bus.*

I know. It's a bus. But I keep thinking about it.

The last time I did the headphones-in-not-listening thing — which is most mornings, the shuffle and forget, the ambient noise as a buffer — it was February, probably, the entry I almost wrote in my head about Ben. Not paying attention and then the back of someone's head and the lurch and the arrival of the brain saying: *no, not him.* And that became a whole thing about the call I hadn't made. And I was staring at my phone the whole time.

Now it's November. I made the call. I booked the house. I said the thing to the ceiling at 10:30pm and she said *I know* and the room stayed the same room. Something is different, not fixed, nothing is finished, but different enough that I can sit on a bus without immediately needing the phone to be between me and whatever's around me.

Is that what it is? I've been turning this over since Tuesday and I think that might be it. The headphones were always a buffer. Not music, just buffer. A signal to the world and also to myself: I am in a private thing, do not arrive, I am handling something internally and the commute is where that happens. And it works, it absolutely functions as described, twenty-two minutes to run whatever loop I'm running, the checkpoint, the nod, the conference room, the alarm, the ceiling. The bus has been my best lapping venue for months.

But Tuesday I didn't press play and I just — was on a bus. A bus with people in it going places. And the sky was doing that undecided gray and the steam came off the coffee cart and the two women at 14th Street were talking and the kid had the paperback and none of it needed me to do anything with it, it was just happening and I was in it.

I almost took my headphones out on Wednesday too. Then didn't. Old habit. The hands know what to do before the head catches up. But Thursday I did it again, the no-play thing, and I watched the city go past and thought about the porch in November and thought about Ben and the call I'm going to make this week and thought about Avery saying *more here* like it was a thing she'd been waiting for without exactly waiting, just — noting when it happened.

*More here.* Maybe that's the shift. Maybe I've been slightly less here, on the bus and elsewhere, for long enough that the no-headphones thing is just what *more here* looks like in the morning. Less buffer, more window.

The plant has seven leaves and I stopped counting as a thing I do. I just water it now. I moved it toward the light when it seemed like the thing to do and apparently it was. Somewhere along the way the tending became ordinary and stopped being something I monitored for signs of my failure. I water it. It grows. I don't know when that became the whole relationship but it did.

Maybe the bus is the same thing. Maybe I've been treating the commute like a problem to manage, same as the document, same as the nod, same as the sentence I was carrying around for eight months while Avery was just there, on the other side of the room, not keeping score. And at some point managing it is just how you do the thing, until you forget to press play one morning and look up and there's a whole bus full of people and a sky and steam and a kid reading a book, and you think: oh, I've been riding past this for a year and a half.

Friday morning I didn't bring the headphones at all. Left them on the counter on purpose, to see if I'd panic. I didn't. I got on the bus and got the window seat and watched the city do its thing for twenty-two minutes and thought, mostly, about nothing. Not the next deliverable. Not Reese. Not whether Drew's nod meant the thing I needed it to mean. Just the buildings and the light and the man with the ambiguous briefcase reading something on his phone.

At one point the two women at 14th Street laughed at something at the same time, really laughed, and the whole bus got a little warmer.

I got off at my stop and walked to the office and made the terrible coffee in the bad-light kitchen and the day started doing what days do.

Tonight I'm going to call Ben. From the block if it's not too cold. Walk around the apartment building once or twice the way I did in February and say: *hey, I didn't ask enough last time. What's actually going on with the apartment thing.* No performance version first. Just that.

The headphones are on the counter where I left them Friday. I haven't put them back in the regular spot. I'm not making a thing of that. I'm just leaving them there for now.

entries.prompt: What's something you said no to recently — and when the moment was over, did you feel relieved, or was there a part of you that wished you'd answered differently? Walk me through the decision.
entries.content:
Priya asked if I wanted to get on a call Thursday night. Not the group chat, a direct message, just to me. She said she'd been listening to my voice memos more than she'd said anything about and she wanted to actually talk, when was I free. And I said — I said I was swamped this week, I'd let her know.

That's the no. Not a hard no. Not a door-slam. Just the soft version where you put something in the future and the future absorbs it.

I've been sitting with it since Thursday and I still don't know which feeling is the real one.

Part of it was true. The week has been real. The new deliverable that announced itself the Monday after the checkpoint, right on schedule, exactly the way I told Nat it would, the hallway extending itself. I've been in the document most evenings and I'm not behind exactly but I'm not comfortable either, and Thursday I had a thing I needed to finish before Friday morning and I could feel the edge of the evening and I knew the call would be two hours minimum because it's Priya, because that's how Priya talks, you don't get Priya in under ninety minutes and that's one of the best things about her and also the thing I was calculating against the clock.

So there was a real reason.

But I noticed something. When I typed *swamped this week, I'll let you know,* the thing I felt was not relief. That's the thing. If it had been pure logistics — genuinely too much, genuinely needed the time — I think I would have felt some small release. That slightly guilty but functional feeling of protecting something. I didn't feel that. I felt the other one. The one that's more like putting something down on a high shelf where you technically can get to it but you have to make the effort.

Priya said she'd been listening to my voice memos more than she'd said anything about.

I keep returning to that sentence. *More than she'd said anything about.* Which means she's heard something in the memos, in my four-minute responses to her twenty-three-minute ones, and she's been sitting with it and deciding whether to say something, and then she decided to and she said *I want to actually talk* and I said *I'm swamped.*

What I think I was actually saying no to was being seen by someone who might be seeing more than I thought. Ben knew me differently — February call, the settled thing, I've been working with that one for months. Nat asks the question underneath and I've started to be able to answer it. But Priya has been in the voice memos in a way I didn't register as two-directional until Thursday. She's been listening and not saying, and then she said something, and I — deflected.

*Why. You've been wanting this. You've been writing for months about the thinning and the wanting-to-talk and the friendship coasting on itself. She handed you the thing.*

I know. I know that.

The part I keep getting stuck on is the *more than she'd said anything about.* Because it means there's been a version of Priya listening to me for weeks or months and forming thoughts about what she heard and not sending them yet, and then she decided to send them, and the decision to send them means she saw something she wanted to respond to, and I genuinely don't know what it is. And that — not knowing what she heard, what she registered, what specifically made her send that message on a Thursday at 8pm — that scared me. More than the two hours. More than the deliverable.

Because what if it's the thing I've been hiding. The laps, the alarm, the managed voice, the whole maintenance operation I run between the real version and what comes out. What if Priya, three time zones away, with her too-long voice memos and her habit of processing everything out loud, has been listening and hearing the thing I haven't said.

*That's what you'd want. You've been wanting someone to hear it.*

Yeah but wanting someone to hear it and someone actually hearing it are different propositions. Wanting is safe. Having heard is irreversible.

I'm going to call her. I already know I'm going to call her. Not Thursday, not *I'll let you know* becoming another plan in the pile — this week, before November, before the porch and the fireplace and all of us in the same place having to be the people we actually are. I want to talk to her first. I want to know what she heard.

And maybe also I just want to talk to Priya for two hours. That's in there too. Underneath the calculation, underneath the Thursday clock and the deliverable edge, there's the actual fact of Priya, who is one of my real people, who sends voice memos about coworkers I'll never meet and means every word of them, who said *I want to actually talk* like it was simple because for Priya it probably is simple, it's probably just a thing you do when you want a thing.

I called Ben Wednesday. Walked around the building twice in the cold, hands in my pockets, and said: *what's actually going on with the apartment thing.* And he told me. Actually told me. It's complicated, the landlord situation, some possibility of having to move by January and not knowing yet if he has to, and he's been carrying it by himself mostly and he'd given me the surface in February and then I'd given him the surface back and we'd been handing each other surfaces for months and he told me the real thing on a cold Wednesday sidewalk and I just listened and then asked a question and then listened more. At the end he said: *thanks for asking. Specifically.* Like he'd noticed I'd asked the specific thing instead of the general thing. *How are you doing* versus *what's actually going on.*

I want to ask Priya the specific thing. I want to tell her the specific thing.

The no on Thursday was real and was also not entirely the reason. I've been doing that accounting more carefully lately, figuring out how much of a decision is logistics and how much is the alarm. Thursday was maybe sixty-forty. Enough real reason to feel justified, enough alarm to make the relief not come.

The deliverable is what it is. I'll close the laptop at seven some evening this week and call her before I can run the math.

She said *I want to actually talk.* That sentence is still sitting in the direct message and I haven't responded since Thursday. She hasn't followed up. She'll wait. Priya has a lot of patience under the voice memos, more than she lets on.

I'm going to tell her about the ceiling at 10:30. About the bus without the headphones. About booking the house before I could stop myself, about standing at the window on a gray-blue Saturday morning with the coffee going cold in my hand and the pigeon doing its confident walk across the fire escape rail.

I'm going to tell her she was right to ask.

entries.prompt: What did you say to yourself the last time you made a mistake at work — walk me through the exact words, and where the conversation went from there.
entries.content:
Left a number wrong in the deliverable.

Not a typo, not a formatting thing — a wrong number. An actual figure that I'd pulled from an older version of a file and didn't catch when I updated everything else, and it sat there in the document, incorrect, for four days before I found it this morning when I was cross-referencing something for a different reason entirely.

The first thing I said, out loud, in the bathroom where I'd taken my laptop to have two minutes without anyone around me, was: *you absolute idiot.*

Then nothing for a second. Then: *Drew has already seen it. Drew has had this number in his head for four days. Who else has seen it. Did this go anywhere. Does this change anything. How did you not check this. You checked everything. You went through that document a hundred times at 1am and you missed this. Of course you missed this. Of course the thing you missed is the thing that looks like you don't know what you're doing.*

Then the larger one, the one that arrived like a second wave: *this is going to be what he remembers. Not the checkpoint, not the* you got there, *not any of it. This is the one that sticks. This is how you find out the nod wasn't real.*

I sat on the closed toilet lid for maybe four minutes having this conversation with myself and then I washed my hands out of habit and went back to my desk.

The correction took eight minutes. I fixed the number, updated the document, added a note in the version history. Then I sat there and looked at the corrected file and thought about what to do next, which should have been obvious, which was immediately tell Drew, and instead I opened the document a second time and read the whole section again as if the number might have fixed itself wrong somehow. It hadn't. It was right now. I had fixed it. The logical next step was still Drew.

I drafted the message four times.

Version one was very long and explained the whole thing, how it happened, which file the number came from, why it was a reasonable mistake in context. I deleted it because it read like someone building a defense before they've been accused of anything.

Version two was very short. Too short. Looked like I was hoping he wouldn't fully register it.

Version three said *sorry* in the first sentence, which I'd heard somewhere you're not supposed to lead with apologizing in professional contexts, so I deleted that too and then sat there for a minute thinking about the fact that I was workshopping an apology for a mistake instead of just — correcting the record.

Version four was: *Hey Drew — caught an error in the deliverable, wrong figure in the third section, pulled from an older file. I've updated the document. Flagging in case it touched anything downstream.*

I sent it.

Then I put my phone face down on the desk and went to get coffee I didn't want and stood in the bad-light kitchen for five minutes and conducted the rest of the conversation with myself.

*You're going to lose the nod over this.* That's where it went. Not the number — the number is fixed, the number is a known and documented and corrected thing — but what the number says about me. What Drew will now have added to the running file he keeps in his head about who I am and whether I'm someone worth the nod. The checkpoint happened, the *you got there* happened, and now there's this, and in my head I was already watching the ledger, watching the *you got there* shrink against the wrong number, running the math on whether one good thing and one mistake nets positive or negative or whether it just resets everything back to unproven.

*It was one number. In one document. That you found and fixed and disclosed.* Yeah.

*Reese would not have made this mistake.* I don't actually know that.

*Yes you do.* Reese's structures are clean. Reese double-checks. Reese is someone who waits for the scope document and you are someone who says yes before the question is done and then misses a figure in a file you'd looked at a hundred times.

I stood there with the bad coffee until the voice wound down, which took longer than it should have. Then I went back to my desk.

Drew responded in about twenty minutes. The message said: *thanks for catching it and looping me in. Doesn't affect anything downstream, just our internal tracking. Good eyes.*

Good eyes.

I stared at that for a while.

*Good eyes.* As if I'd spotted something in someone else's work. As if I were being commended for a catch and not implicated in a mistake. Drew either genuinely read it as me finding an error in the document rather than in my own work — which seems unlikely — or he read it exactly right and decided that flagging it was the thing that mattered and not how the error arrived there. And either way that's what I got. *Good eyes.*

I thought about the checkpoint. *You got there.* I thought about the version of me that, three weeks ago, would have taken the wrong number and either buried it or spent twelve hours catastrophizing before saying anything. I found it, fixed it, sent four drafts of a message and then sent the real one, and the world continued to be the world.

The voice in the bathroom was so sure. So certain that this was the one, the referendum, the moment when Drew would do the math and I'd come up short. And instead: *good eyes.* Doesn't affect anything downstream.

I don't know how to fully receive that. I'm still working on it. There's a part of me that wants to treat *good eyes* as a performance, as Drew being professionally gracious while privately updating the ledger against me. That part is persistent and not entirely trustworthy and I know that about it and it's still there.

But I keep thinking about Avery. About *I know* from the dark. About Nat saying *everyone could see it but you.* About the evidence I've been collecting and not filing.

This is more evidence. I made a mistake and disclosed it and the thing that followed was *good eyes, doesn't affect anything downstream.* The story I was telling myself in the bathroom — the ledger, the nod reversing, this is the one that sticks — that story did not happen. A different thing happened. A pretty quiet thing, actually. A thing that required me to do the reasonable next step and then let the response be what it was.

The voice was so loud. Four minutes on a toilet lid. Four drafts. And what it was protecting me from turned out to be a twenty-minute wait and two words.

I need to call Priya tonight. I said this week and it's already Wednesday and I want to tell her about the bathroom and the four drafts and *good eyes* and how loud the voice gets over something that turns out to be a quiet thing. I want to tell her what I said to myself and where the conversation went from there.

She's been listening to the voice memos. She wanted to actually talk.

I think she probably already knows the shape of this. I think she's been waiting for me to see it too.

entries.prompt: When did you last do something generous for someone — and was there a moment, even briefly, where you wished you hadn't? Walk me through what happened.
entries.content:
Avery had a brutal week. That's the frame for this. A thing at Avery's job that I don't fully understand but have been watching accumulate since Monday — the way she'd come home and be fine for about twenty minutes and then go somewhere inside herself, the look that was present-but-absent, the kind of tired that sleep doesn't fix. By Thursday she wasn't talking about it anymore, which is somehow worse than talking about it.

So Friday I planned a whole thing.

Not a grand gesture, nothing that required documentation. I looked up the restaurant she'd mentioned twice in the last month without us ever going — some place a friend had been, small, not expensive, the kind of thing you mention and then don't push on. Made a reservation for Saturday. Got home Friday, cleaned the apartment, which needed it and also I wanted it clean before she came over. Bought flowers, which I've never done, which required me to stand in front of a bodega display for about three minutes having no idea what I was doing, and the guy behind the counter said *yellow ones* with an authority that I accepted entirely. Came home with yellow flowers and put them in the only thing I had that resembles a vase, which is a wide-mouthed jar that used to have pasta sauce in it, and they looked fine, they looked actually fine, and I felt something that was uncomplicated and good. The apartment was clean. There were flowers. I had a plan.

Avery came over and I showed her the reservation and she said *oh* in this small way and then looked at me for a second, like she was recalibrating something, and said thank you. That was the moment. That was the thing I'd wanted, that specific look, and I had it.

And somewhere between that look and us actually leaving for dinner I felt it. The other thing. Small and fast, not proud of it, but it was there.

*What if this is what I'm for now.*

Not a thought I chose. Just the one that arrived. What if I've been quietly filing myself under *the person who notices when Avery is struggling and does the thing about it*, and now that's its own kind of performance, its own version of the managed voice, the careful architecture of being someone who is easy to be with. What if the flowers and the clean apartment and the restaurant Avery mentioned twice are me running the same operation I run with Drew — anticipating what's wanted, producing it, waiting for the nod. Just pointed in a different direction.

I watched myself think that and immediately wanted to take it back. It's a terrible thought. The kind the voice produces when things are going well and it needs something to do.

Dinner was good. Actually good, not performed good. The food was the kind of thing where you don't talk for a minute after it arrives because you're both just eating and that's the only response. Avery told me more about the work thing, the real version, not the surface she'd been handing me all week, and I listened and asked the specific question and listened more. At one point she reached across the table and touched my hand, not for long, just briefly, and then pulled it back and kept talking. I almost couldn't follow what she was saying after that. I was still somewhere in that small moment.

Walking home she said: *this was really good. Thank you for doing this.*

And I said: *I wanted to.*

Which is true. Completely true. The other thought was real too and it exists and I don't get to disqualify it by being uncomfortable with what it says about me. Both things were there at the same time — I genuinely wanted to do something for her, and I also briefly wondered if I was turning intimacy into a project, something to execute well so I could feel like I'd succeeded at it.

I've been thinking about why that second thing came. Walking home, I mean, I was already thinking about it while she was still talking. And here's what I keep arriving at: it's hard to do something purely generous when you're also afraid of losing the thing. When you love something a lot — and I know what I want, I filed that evidence, I said the ceiling thing — when you love something a lot, the generous acts start to carry a second weight you didn't ask them to carry. Not manipulation. Not calculation. Just fear wearing the wrong coat. *I want to make her happy* and *I'm scared she'll stop wanting this* are not the same sentence and somehow in the bodega with the yellow flowers they got tangled up together.

The thought didn't ruin anything. Dinner was still good. The hand on mine was still the hand on mine. She told me the real version of the work thing and I got to actually hear it. That's not nothing. That's the thing I wanted.

But I keep going back to the thought because the voice in the bathroom over a corrected number was four minutes and four drafts and then *good eyes*. And this was one bad thought in the middle of a night that was genuinely good and I'm still carrying it two days later like it's the verdict.

I told Avery about it this morning. Not the full analysis, not the whole essay I've been running in my head — just the bare version. I said: *I had a weird moment last night. After you said thank you. I had this thought about whether I was doing something real or just trying to be the right kind of person.*

She was quiet for a second. Then: *what made you wonder?*

I said: *I think when I care about something a lot I get confused about what I'm doing. Whether it's for you or whether it's for some picture of myself I'm trying to be.*

She looked at me for a while. Not the recalibrating look — something more like the ceiling in the dark, the *I know*, the specific patience that I keep not accounting for. She said: *I think the fact that you're asking that probably means it was real.*

I don't know if she's right. But I said *okay* and meant it, which is the thing I've been practicing. Let the response be what it is. Don't wait for the catch.

The flowers are still in the jar on the windowsill. Two of them are drooping a little. The plant has seven leaves and one of the older ones is going slightly yellow at the edge, which I looked up and apparently is normal, apparently leaves do that, apparently you don't have to treat every yellow edge as an indictment.

Yellow flowers in a pasta sauce jar next to a seven-leaved plant with a slightly yellow leaf. That's what's on my windowsill.

It'll do. It more than does.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been looking forward to telling someone — good news, a small win, anything — that you've ended up keeping to yourself? Walk me through who you almost told, and what stopped you.
entries.content:
The sixth leaf grew in while I wasn't watching. I know I've said that before about the plant but I keep thinking about it in a different context now, which is: I got a callback.

Second round. The kind of thing where they want you to come in and meet the actual team, not just the recruiter on a video call. I got the email Wednesday at 4:47pm and I read it twice and put my phone down and looked at my screen for maybe a full minute doing nothing.

I haven't told anyone.

Not Avery. Not Nat. Not Ben, who I've been calling more often now and who I could have called Wednesday night when I was walking around the block with my hands in my pockets because I needed somewhere to put the energy. I walked for twenty minutes and didn't call anyone and came back in and said I needed air when Avery asked where I'd gone.

The job is something I applied to six weeks ago, on a night when I was particularly deep in the hallway feeling — the *once the checkpoint is done I'll breathe* thing, except the checkpoint was done and I'd started reassigning the weight to the next deliverable on schedule, the way I told Nat I would. I sent the application at 11pm on a Tuesday and then felt stupid about it and forgot I'd done it in the way you forget things you've half-decided not to want. And then there was a phone screen in October that I almost canceled because I'd talked myself down from wanting it by then, and I did it, and it went fine, and I told myself fine meant nothing and forgot about it again.

The callback arrived and I apparently still want it. That's the thing. The wanting was still there, intact, just where I'd left it.

*You should tell Avery.*

I know that. Obviously Avery. Avery is the person I'm building the habit of telling things to, this is explicitly the project, I have evidence that saying true things to Avery produces *I know* and *you're more interesting when you stop trying to seem like you have it together* and a hand across a table at a restaurant I planned. Avery is not going to disappear because I applied for a job.

Here's what stopped me.

It's a different company. Different city, maybe — the role is listed as hybrid but the office is somewhere that's not here. I don't know what it pays yet. I don't know if I'd actually take it. I know almost nothing except that I sent an application at 11pm on a Tuesday because I was in the hallway and I needed to believe there were other hallways. And now it's real enough that they want to meet me, and if I tell Avery about it I have to tell Avery all of that. The 11pm application. The maybe-different-city. The *I did this thing and I didn't tell you I was doing it.* I have to explain six weeks of quiet in a conversation I'm not prepared to have.

*More than she'd said anything about.* That's Priya's sentence. I keep borrowing it. I've been keeping this more than I've said anything about, and not because I'm hiding it, not exactly, but because admitting it means admitting I didn't know what it was when I did it and I still don't fully know. Is it wanting something different. Is it just the static, the lapping, the energy looking for a new container. Is it the version of me that needed to believe there were other hallways even if I don't want to walk down them. I don't know and I don't want to have that conversation until I know, except I've been learning lately that *until I know* is another way of saying never.

Wednesday night on the block I got as far as composing the sentence. *I got a callback for something I applied to.* Stood there in the cold with it in my mouth and then swallowed it and walked home.

Thursday I almost told Reese. Which is its own weird thing. Reese and I have been — not friends, that's not the right word, but something warmer than coworkers since the document situation, since the good collaboration thing, since we started occasionally getting the bad coffee at the same time and talking about nothing specific for four minutes. I almost said, in the kitchen with the bad overhead light: *I might be interviewing somewhere.* The logic being that Reese doesn't have the stakes Avery has, Reese is a trial run, a smaller venue. 

I didn't. Partly because it felt disloyal to Drew in some way I couldn't fully articulate, and partly because if I say it to Reese first I'm definitely doing the wrong version of the thing.

Nat would be the right call. Nat who asks the question that goes underneath. Nat who would say *so what is it you actually want* and wait while I figured out whether I could answer it. Except I've been calling Nat more and the calls have been good and I don't want this to be the thing where I use Nat to process something I should be saying to Avery. That's its own pattern. The Nat call as a way of getting my story straight before I have to tell the person it actually affects.

So nobody. I've told no one and I've been carrying it for five days and it's getting heavy in the way things get heavy when they're not moving anywhere.

*The unperformed version was okay.* I wrote that last week about the wrong number. Drew said *good eyes* and the ledger didn't reset. Avery said *I know* in the dark and the room stayed the same room. I have this whole inventory of moments where I said the true thing and something okay followed it and I keep adding to the inventory without updating what I do in the next situation that needs a true thing said.

The callback is Friday. The meeting, I mean — they want a Friday afternoon slot and I said yes and now I have a calendar block that says *personal* and nothing else and I've been looking at it all week.

I'm going to tell Avery tonight. Not the whole essay. Just: *I applied for something six weeks ago and I got further than I expected and I don't know what I want and I needed to tell you before it got any heavier.* Let her ask the questions. Answer them as honestly as I can. See what follows.

And I know — I know — that what will follow is probably patience. The particular Avery patience I keep failing to account for. The *I know* and the *I'm not going anywhere* and the specific look of someone who has been waiting, without exactly waiting, for me to put things down in front of them.

I might still want to stay here. In this city, in this apartment, in this configuration. Nothing about the callback changes the ceiling at 10:30 or the Saturday morning coffee or the yellow flowers in the pasta sauce jar. The wanting is still where I filed it.

But I applied for something at 11pm on a Tuesday and I need Avery to know that I'm a person who does that. Not just the checkpoint person, the plant person, the guy who books the November house and says the real thing eventually. Also the person who sometimes gets in the hallway and needs to believe there's another way out, even when they're not sure they want to take it.

That's the whole person. That's the one I keep trying to introduce.

Tonight. Before the words get workshopped into something easier.

entries.prompt: When's the last time you were in a conversation and realized, partway through, that you were performing a version of yourself that didn't quite fit the moment — what were you saying, and what were you actually thinking?
entries.content:
Told Avery last night.

Not tonight — last night, before I'd written this, before I'd finished deciding. I'd been in the kitchen making dinner and she came in and asked what I wanted to watch later and something about the question, the simple domesticity of it, the fact that she was just standing there in the doorframe asking what I wanted to watch — I said: *I got a callback. For a job I applied to six weeks ago. I didn't tell you I applied.*

She was quiet. Not the bad quiet. The putting-it-down-carefully quiet.

And I started talking, and somewhere in the first two minutes I noticed I had shifted into the wrong version.

That's the thing I want to write about. Not the conversation itself — the conversation was okay, it was actually okay, I'll get there — but the thing that happened in the middle of it where I caught myself performing and couldn't immediately stop.

I was explaining the application. The Tuesday at 11pm, the checkpoint hallway feeling, the *I needed to believe there were other options* framing. All accurate. But I could hear myself smoothing it as I went, making it coherent in a way it hadn't been when I was actually doing it, giving it a narrative shape that made me sound like someone who had made a considered exploratory decision rather than someone who filled out a form late at night because the walls were doing the thing where they get closer. I was constructing a reasonable person in real time and presenting him to Avery and the reasonable person was mostly me but not entirely me and I knew it while I was doing it.

*You're editorializing. She asked what happened and you're giving her the version where you had reasons.*

I kept going for another full sentence and a half before I stopped.

And then I stopped. Mid-sentence, actually mid-sentence, and Avery looked at me.

I said: *I'm doing the thing. I'm making it sound more intentional than it was. I just needed to feel like there was a door somewhere and I went looking for one and now there might actually be a door and I'm scared to want it and I'm scared not to want it and I haven't told you for five days because I didn't know how to say that without it being a whole thing.*

She didn't say anything for a second.

Then: *that's the version I wanted to hear.*

And something in my chest did the heavy-thing-being-put-down feeling. Not relief exactly. The other one. Arrival.

What I was actually thinking during the smooth version — the reasonable-person construction, the considered-exploratory-decision framing — was something more like: *she is going to hear 'different city, maybe' and do the math and I need to get ahead of the math, I need her to know I'm not going anywhere before she has a chance to wonder, I need to perform someone who has already thought this through so she doesn't have to worry about what I haven't thought through.* Which is the old operation. The managed voice, pointed in a different direction. Avery as Drew. The nod I needed, re-skinned.

I notice it faster now than I used to. That's something. The gap between starting the performance and catching it has gotten smaller. It used to be — the whole Slack message, the whole conference room, the whole *I've got room for it* before the brain arrived to say *what did you just do.* Now it's about a sentence and a half. That's real progress in a direction I didn't know I was traveling.

The conversation after I stopped smoothing it was better. Harder in places but better. She asked about the role, what it actually was, what drew me to it at 11pm on a Tuesday. I said I wasn't sure I knew, which was true, and she nodded like that was an acceptable answer. She asked about the city situation — the office, the hybrid thing, the maybe-not-here part. I said I didn't know enough yet to know what it would actually mean. She said: *okay. What do you want to find out on Friday?*

Which was such an Avery question. Not *are you leaving* or *what does this mean for us* — just: what do you want to find out. Move it forward. See what's there.

I said I wanted to know if the thing I applied for at 11pm was something I'd actually want in the daylight.

She said that seemed like a reasonable thing to find out.

We ate dinner. The thing I'd been making got slightly overdone while I was standing there confessing to a job application but it was fine, it was edible, and we sat at the table and she asked me what the role actually was and I described it and she asked a smart question about it that I hadn't thought about and I said I hadn't thought about that and she said well maybe think about it before Friday. And that was it. That was the whole thing. The conversation I'd workshopped for five days and declined to have and finally had in approximately thirteen minutes over overdone pasta.

The smooth version would have landed differently. I know that. The smooth version might have technically conveyed the same information but Avery would have been talking to the constructed reasonable person instead of the actual one, and the actual one is who she said she wanted, back when she said *you're more interesting when you stop trying to seem like you have it together.* She wasn't asking for the rough edges as a preference. She just could already tell the difference.

That's the thing I'm still absorbing. She could already tell the difference. When I switched from the smooth version to the stopped-mid-sentence version, she said *that's the version I wanted to hear* — which means she'd been listening to the smooth version and knowing it was the smooth version and waiting. Not impatiently. Just — knowing. Like she's always known, like the *I know* in the dark was about this too and not just the other thing.

I don't know what Friday will tell me. The role might be wrong. The people might be wrong. I might walk in and feel nothing and come home and close that tab and move on. Or I might feel something and not know what to do with it and have to have another thirteen-minute conversation over whatever's on the stove. Either way I'm going to tell Avery the real version, the Tuesday-at-11pm version, not the constructed one. I know how to do that now. I practiced last night, in a sentence and a half.

Friday. Then I find out if the door is actually a door or just what a wall looks like sometimes when the light is low and you need somewhere to go.

The pasta sauce jar still has two flowers in it. The drooping ones. They're almost done but they haven't fallen over yet and the plant has seven leaves and I'm leaving both of them alone.

entries.prompt:
The last few entries have been about the job, Avery, performing versus feeling — all circling the same live wire. Time to pull somewhere else.

The user has written about the bus as a habit they felt differently about (Mar 20), and about Nat (Mar 21), and about Ben (mentioned in passing in the callback entry but never explored). The callback / Avery thread has been the dominant one for several entries running. A good place to go: something outside that cluster — maybe something about a person or relationship that's been quietly present but not front-and-center. Ben has come up twice without ever being the subject of a prompt.

---

You've been calling Ben more lately — walk me through the last actual conversation: what you talked about on the surface, and what, if anything, you didn't say.
entries.content:
Called him Thursday. Seven-something, after dinner, before I'd fully committed to the couch. I'd been meaning to for a week and Thursday was one of those nights where the evening hadn't organized itself into anything yet and I had the thought and then just — did it. Didn't run the version first. Dialed before I could start preparing.

He picked up on the second ring, which Ben always does, like he's been waiting for whoever calls.

We talked about the apartment situation first. His landlord came through with the offer to renew, which is technically good news except the rent is up seventeen percent and he's doing the math on whether staying makes sense versus finding somewhere new in January in a city where finding somewhere new in January is its own particular punishment. He's been looking at listings in a way that's started to feel compulsive, he said — not because he wants to move, just because knowing what else is out there makes the decision feel less like something being done to him and more like something he's choosing. Or not choosing. He's not sure.

I said that made sense. Then I said: I did something like that six weeks ago. Applied for a job I wasn't sure I wanted, mostly to feel like there was a door.

I hadn't planned to say it. It just arrived in that order, attached to what he'd said about the listings. He was quiet for a second and then he said: *did you hear back?*

I told him about the callback. The Friday meeting, which is tomorrow now, which I've been aware of like a sound in the wall that I can't locate. He asked what the role was. I described it. He asked if I wanted it.

I said I didn't know yet.

He said: *what did Avery say?*

Which — it mattered, that he asked that. That his first question was Avery. A year ago, six months ago even, he would have asked about the money or the title or how it compared to what I'm doing now, the practical architecture of it. The fact that he went to Avery first meant he was tracking something. Knew something about where things had moved without me explaining the whole trajectory.

I told him Avery had been good about it. That I'd told her the real version, the Tuesday-at-11pm version, not the constructed one. He said that was good. Said it simply, just: *that's good.* And I could hear in the way he said it that he'd been watching this from wherever he was — the group chat, the calls, the spaces between things — and had his own read on what *that's good* meant and I didn't need to explain it.

That's the thing about Ben. The shorthand comes back. Not all of it — there are places where a reference lands and I have to wait a half-beat to see if we both remember why it's funny, and sometimes we do and sometimes there's a small gap — but the underneath layer, the way he listens, the specific timing of when he says something and when he lets a thing breathe — that came back immediately. Like a physical reflex. I forgot I'd been worried about the gap.

On the surface we talked about his apartment, my callback, a thing his coworker did that was objectively absurd. He did an impression of the coworker. I laughed harder than I expected. He asked about the plant because I'd mentioned it on a previous call and he'd remembered, which is a Ben thing, he catalogues details like that, and I said seven leaves now, the yellow one at the edge is stabilizing. He said: *you didn't kill it.* I said: *not yet.* He said: *yet* in a voice that meant something fond.

We talked for almost an hour.

Here's what I didn't say.

I didn't say that when he told me about the landlord, about the listings, about the seventeen percent — I felt this thing underneath the sympathy that was almost like recognition. Not of his situation exactly, but of the feeling of standing in something that's basically okay and still scanning for the exit because you don't fully trust the okay. That's what the listings are for him, I think. Not a plan. Just proof that he still has options. I know that feeling specifically. I've been doing a version of it for months. I didn't say that because it would have required me to diagnose something he hadn't asked to have diagnosed, and also because I wasn't totally sure I was right, and also because sometimes recognition is not what's needed, sometimes what's needed is someone who asks about the rent and helps run the numbers.

I didn't say that the *you seem more settled* thing from February has been living in my head for almost a year. That I've been carrying it around as a small piece of evidence against myself, proof that the surface was holding while the inside was still doing laps, proof that even Ben couldn't tell. I think I understand now that he meant it warmly and meant it accurately — that something about me was quieter, or less visibly unmoored, and that was what he saw from where he was sitting. I'm less bothered by it. I don't know that I needed to say so. But it was there, in the background of the call, this thing I'd been holding for months that I kept not bringing up and didn't bring up again.

I didn't say I'm glad we figured out the calling. That I'd been scared for a while that the call would tell me the gap was bigger than I could stand, that we'd both become people in different directions and the shorthand would be gone and there'd be this polite, thin version of the friendship where we said we should do the trip and didn't. I didn't say I was scared of that and it didn't happen. You don't usually say the thing you were afraid of after you find out there was nothing to be afraid of. You just move forward. But it was there.

At the end, right before we hung up — this is always where the real thing lands with Ben, we save it for last — he said: *let me know how Friday goes.* 

That was it. Not *good luck* or *hope it goes well.* Just: let me know. Which means he wants to know. Which means he'll be somewhere on the other side of Friday with a tab open.

I said I would.

Then he said: *you're going to be fine, by the way. Whatever you decide.*

I said yeah. Probably.

He said: *definitely.* In the voice that means he's not entirely convinced either but he's saying it for real, not just as punctuation, and he wants me to hear it that way.

I think I did.

Tomorrow is Friday. The door or the wall with the light hitting it wrong.

Either way I'm telling him what happened. That's what *let me know* means. That's the whole point of having figured out the calling.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been telling yourself you're fine about — and when did you last catch yourself acting like you're not?
entries.content:
Friday happened.

I'm fine about it.

That's the sentence I've been saying since I got off the bus this afternoon. On the bus, to myself, looking out the window at the city doing its city thing. Walking from the stop. Putting my key in the door. *I'm fine about it.* Present tense, declarative, no asterisk.

The meeting was good. The people were good. The role made sense in a way that surprised me, in the room, more than it had on paper at 11pm on a Tuesday six weeks ago. There was a moment, about forty minutes in, when the hiring manager was describing what the team was building toward and I caught myself actually leaning forward — not performed interest, not the eight-minute Slack message version of interest, just my body moving toward a thing before I'd approved the movement. I noticed it and then tried not to think about whether they'd noticed it because that's the road to ruin.

They said they'd be in touch early next week.

I'm fine about it. Totally fine. Great meeting, interesting role, we'll see.

Here's when I caught myself acting like I'm not fine about it: twenty minutes ago, standing at the kitchen counter eating crackers out of the box because I couldn't figure out what I actually wanted to eat and the crackers were just there. I'd been standing there for probably ten minutes. The crackers were not a decision. The crackers were what happens when some part of me is running so many background processes that the foreground shuts down and I'm just standing in my kitchen at 6pm on a Friday putting crackers in my mouth and staring at the backsplash.

Avery is coming over later. We're supposed to figure out dinner. I have not figured out anything. I am in the crackers phase.

I called Ben from the block before I came up. Told him it was good. Told him about the leaning-forward thing. He said: *so you want it.* Not a question. I said I don't know if that's what the leaning forward means. He said: *what else would it mean.* I said I'd been known to lean forward at things I didn't actually want, that I have a history with this, that I said yes in a conference room because Drew nodded at me and I needed the nod and that's a kind of leaning forward too. He was quiet for a second. Then he said: *that's different and you know it.*

I said yeah. Okay. Maybe.

He said: *what's scary about wanting it.*

Which is the question that goes underneath. I didn't know Nat had been teaching him that. Or maybe he's always been able to do it and I just wasn't in a place to hear it.

I didn't answer on the phone. Said I had to go up, I'd think about it. He said let him know. Same as before. The tab still open.

What's scary about wanting it.

Here's what I didn't say to Ben, what I've been standing in the kitchen with since I came inside. If I want the job — like, actually want it, the way I want things when I let myself want them fully, the way I wanted to stay after the ceiling-at-10:30 conversation — then I have to do something with that wanting. It stops being the application I sent at 11pm and forgot about, the door-as-concept, the proof that options exist. It becomes a real thing with real shape and real implications that I have to sit across from Avery and talk about, not the constructed reasonable-person version, not the smooth one. The actual version, which is: *I went into a room today and I leaned toward something and I don't know what it would mean for us and I need to figure out what I want and also what you need and those might not be the same thing and I'm scared of finding out which way that cuts.*

That's the conversation I haven't had yet. I did the disclosure, I did the Tuesday-at-11pm version, I got credit for the real thing. But that was before there was a real thing. Now there's a real thing and it has an early-next-week timeline and some kind of city situation that we still haven't mapped out and I'm eating crackers at the backsplash like if I stand here long enough the problem will sort itself.

*You're fine about it.* Sure. Completely fine.

The plant has seven leaves and the yellow one at the edge is doing what it's doing and I've stopped monitoring it but I looked at it when I came in today, specifically looked at it, like it was going to tell me something. It didn't tell me anything. It's a plant. It was just there on the windowsill in the late afternoon light and all seven leaves were present and accounted for and I stood there looking at it for probably too long before I remembered I was holding my coat.

I know what Ben's question was actually asking. It wasn't *what specifically frightens you about this professional opportunity.* It was: you've spent eight months learning to want things out loud, learning to say the real version, learning to not redirect before the sentence is done — and now there's a thing that might require you to want something out loud that has stakes, real stakes, not just the checkpoint or the nod or the ceiling, but something that requires another person to also want something and you're not in control of what they want. And that's different. The coffee on Saturday mornings and the hand across the table and *I'm not going anywhere* — none of that prepared me for the version where I might be the one going somewhere. Or not. Or not knowing yet.

I'm fine about it.

I'm standing in my kitchen eating crackers and I've looked at the plant twice and I called Ben and didn't answer the real question and Avery is coming over in about an hour and I haven't figured out dinner and the hiring manager is going to email me early next week and I've been saying *I'm fine about it* since the bus and what I actually am is very much in the middle of something I don't know the shape of yet.

That's what fine means right now. Not okay. Not settled. Just — still standing, crackers in hand, waiting for Avery to knock so I can open the door and figure out how to say the next true thing.

entries.prompt:
The job news is now out in the open — that thread has had its run. Time to go somewhere else.

Looking at the history: Ben got a full entry (Mar 29) but the conversation in that entry ended mid-thought, with the user trailing off after mentioning the application. The *relationship* with Ben — what it actually means to them, how it functions differently from other relationships — hasn't been the subject of a prompt. But that's still close to the job thread.

What's genuinely untouched: the user's relationship with Avery outside of the job secret. The Mar 24 entry was about doing something generous for Avery after a brutal week, and there was ambivalence in it. That's a while ago. The domestic life — what it's actually like day to day — has only appeared as backdrop.

Also untouched: something lighter or stranger. The Mar 2 entry about laughing at
entries.content:
Saturday, and nothing was scheduled, which used to feel like relief and now sometimes feels like a small test I haven't studied for.

Avery was already up when I woke — I could hear the specific sounds of someone trying to be quiet in a kitchen, which is its own kind of sound, the deliberate softness of it. I lay there for a few minutes just listening. Cabinet, tap, the soft knock of the kettle being set down. I know all of these sounds now. I know which creak is the floorboard by the stove and which one is the step in front of the bathroom and I know the way Avery clears her throat first thing in the morning, this small involuntary sound that she doesn't know she makes, and all of this knowing accumulated without my realizing I was accumulating it. Like the plant leaves. You're not watching and then suddenly you know every sound a person makes in the first twenty minutes of the day.

I got up and went to the kitchen and she handed me coffee without turning around. Knew I was there from the creak, maybe, or just from the rhythm of the morning. I took it and stood beside her and for a while we didn't say anything.

Here's what's been strange this week, though, and I haven't written it down yet: the nothing-scheduled day kept catching me sideways.

We went to the farmer's market in the morning, the one twelve blocks east that takes forty minutes to walk to and is objectively not worth forty minutes of walking because you can get most of the same things two blocks away, but the forty-minute version is outside and the produce is slightly better and we've done it before, it's a known quantity. And I was fine, I was good, we were walking and she was carrying the bag and I had the coffee I'd brought from home in a thermos, and at some point we stopped at a stand with apples and she held one up and said something about a pie and I said I didn't know how to make pie and she said she didn't either but how hard could it be and I said probably not as hard as we think and then we were having a conversation about pie we'd never make and it was one of the easy stupid things, the thing I'd wanted after the real thing, the slide, and I was having it and everything was fine.

And then somewhere between the apple stand and the cheese person — there's a cheese person, there's always a cheese person at these things — I had this thought that arrived without being invited: *what if this is the version that doesn't change.*

Not a bad thought exactly. More like the thought you have when you're holding something that seems too good for the conditions. The way you check whether a thing is real by pressing on it slightly. I was walking next to Avery in the cold, thermos in hand, and I thought: what if this is just — what it is, for a long time, the farmer's market and the Saturday mornings and the cheese person and the knowing what sound the floorboard makes.

The job. The maybe-different-city. The early-next-week email that hasn't arrived yet and is three days late now and might mean nothing or might mean they went another way. I've been carrying it like something I haven't put down because there's nowhere obvious to put it, and the not-putting-it-down means it's there at the apple stand when I'm supposed to be having the easy stupid conversation about pie.

I didn't say any of this at the market. I made the pie conversation happen and it happened genuinely, I wasn't performing it, but there was this extra layer present that I was choosing not to add to the transaction.

Avery bought the apples. We didn't buy the stuff to make pie. We got cheese from the cheese person and bread from the bread person and walked the forty minutes back with the bag between us taking turns carrying it, and at some point she put her hand through my arm the way she does when we're walking and not talking, and I thought: *this is the thing I wanted to stay for. You're in it. You're standing right in it.*

And I was. And the job thing was still there too.

After lunch I sat at the table while she was reading in the other room and I tried to figure out what was bothering me, specifically, not generally. Because it's not the job. The job is a door that may or may not be a door. That's fine, that's manageable, I've sat with not-knowing for three weeks and I can keep sitting with it. What's been harder is this: I want two things and I don't think wanting two things is supposed to feel like a contradiction but it does. I want the Saturday mornings and the forty-minute farmer's market and the knowing of all the sounds. I want to lean toward something in a room and have that mean something about where I'm going. And those two things might be compatible — probably are, actually, probably this is something people navigate and it turns out fine — but in the part of my chest where I run the operations, they've been sitting on opposite sides of a line and I haven't figured out how to move the line.

I haven't told Avery I'm still waiting on the email. She knows I'm waiting. She hasn't asked. Which is — I don't know if that's her giving me room or her also being in the not-knowing and not wanting to press on it yet. We haven't pressed on it since the overdone pasta conversation and the week has just been the week: the bus, the work, the evenings, the Saturday farmer's market with the apples. Good, mostly. The refrigerator hum underneath.

What I actually want to do, what I've been wanting to do since Thursday, is just tell her: *I'm scared of getting what I want.* Not about the job specifically. About wanting things that have stakes. About the fact that I spent eight months learning to say true things and the true things so far have mostly been about her and now there might be a true thing that requires her to say something back and I can't prep the version of that response in advance and wait for the right moment to send it.

She came out of the other room around four and said she was going to make tea and did I want any. I said yes. She made tea. We sat on the couch and she had her book and I had nothing in particular and it was the good quiet, the non-loaded kind, and I kept the thought where it was, in the chest, just a little longer.

Not forever. Just — today I wanted the easy thing. The farmer's market, the cheese person, the forty minutes back with the bag between us.

Tomorrow I'll put it down in front of her. Or she'll ask and I'll answer without drafting it first. One of those.

The email might come Monday. Or it might have already come and gone and this is already settled in a direction and I just don't know yet. Either way the floorboard creaks where it creaks and the kettle sounds like the kettle and she handed me coffee this morning without turning around because she knew from the creak that I was there.

That's the evidence. It keeps piling up. At some point I have to stop waiting for the catch.

entries.prompt: When's the last time you were somewhere familiar — a place you've been dozens of times — and it felt unexpectedly strange, or different, or like you were seeing it for the first time? Walk me through what happened.
entries.content:
Monday. The coffee shop — the one with the two specific baristas I know, Marcus with the shaved head who always asks how the morning's going and means it, and the one whose name I still don't know after eight months who just hands you the cup already knowing what you ordered and somehow that's better than asking. I go three or four mornings a week. I have a spot near the window I aim for, the one where the heater ticks but works, where you can see the mailbox on the corner and the bus stop bench and the small domestic theater of people waiting for things. I know this place. I know how it smells before they've started really busy, I know the exact squeak of the door and the way the afternoon light comes in different from the morning light. I've probably been in there sixty times. Seventy. I stopped counting because counting is for places you're not sure of yet.

I went this morning to wait. That's what I was doing. The email was supposed to come last week and didn't, which might mean nothing, might mean they went another way and I'm waiting for a rejection that'll arrive apologetically in the third week of the month when everyone's calendar has cleared up. Or it might mean they're still deciding. I've been in the not-knowing long enough that I've gotten fluent in it — every variation of *no news* and what it probably means and the counter-argument to that and the counter to the counter. My brain has been running this as a background process since Friday and this morning I needed somewhere to be where I didn't have to do anything with it, so I went to the coffee shop.

Marcus asked how the morning was going. I said it was good, how was his, he said the usual, he said it in the way that's actually fond rather than routine, and I took my coffee to the window spot and sat down.

And then I just — looked at it.

The place. Like I'd never been there.

I don't know what triggered it. Nothing was different. Same stools, same chalkboard with the specials in the same handwriting, same ticking heater, same light. But I was looking at the room and getting this feeling, distinct and strange, like I'd walked into a memory of somewhere rather than the place itself. Like there was a hair of delay between me and everything in it.

The woman at the table near the door was grading something, red pen, stack of papers with a rubber band around the ungraded ones. I've probably seen her before, she has the look of a regular, but I'd never registered her specifically. The two guys at the counter were talking in the half-voice people use when they're aware of being in a shared space, serious about something, taking turns. The no-name barista was wiping down the espresso machine with the same cloth she always uses and the motion was so practiced it looked almost automatic, like the body doing something it doesn't need the mind for anymore.

*You've been here sixty times and you've never looked at any of them.*

Not looking at my phone, not running the background process, just — the woman with the red pen, the two guys, the automatic motion of the cloth on the machine, the mailbox outside with someone stopped to put something in it, the little effort it takes to lift the heavy door.

I don't know how long I sat like that. Long enough that my coffee went from hot to warm without me drinking much of it. The room kept being the room. Nothing revealed itself or landed with significance. I wasn't having a moment. I was just sitting in a familiar place and finding it unexpectedly full of people who had full mornings that had nothing to do with me.

The email thing was still there. The other thing — Avery, the floorboard, the forty-minute farmer's market — still there. The whole operations center, running. But it was quieter than usual. Like something had turned the volume down two notches while I wasn't managing it.

I think what made the place strange was that I arrived needing it to be a container for something and it just — wasn't that. It was a coffee shop. It had always been a coffee shop. I've been using it as a waiting room, a lap-running venue, a place to be not-somewhere-else, and this morning it declined to be any of those things and was just itself. Seventy visits and this was the first time I looked up and found a room full of people having a morning instead of a backdrop for whatever I was processing.

Marcus warmed up my cup without me asking, the way he does. Set it down and moved on. The no-name barista switched from the espresso machine to the counter and started the same practiced wipe-down and I watched her and thought: she's done this ten thousand times. This exact motion. This cloth on this surface. And at some point that stopped being a task and became just — what her hands do. Like the floorboard creak. Like knowing what the kettle sounds like.

I thought about Avery handing me coffee this morning without turning around.

I thought about Ben saying *you're going to be fine* in the voice that meant he was also saying it for himself, a little, and that was okay, that's what the voice was allowed to be.

I thought about November. The porch. Less than three weeks now.

The email didn't come this morning. I checked when I got to work, and again at lunch, and the thing I noticed is that I checked twice instead of eleven times, which is not a small thing. There's a version of this where the not-knowing gets smaller each day just from existing in it long enough. Where the static turns out to have been temporary all along and I was just in the middle of it, unable to see that it had edges.

I don't know if I want the job. I do know I want to find out what I want, which is different and maybe more honest. I want to get the email, whichever kind it is, and find out what the feeling is. See whether it's relief or the other one, the arrival feeling, and finally have some actual information instead of running probabilities.

But this morning the coffee shop was strange and full of people I've been ignoring for eight months. The woman with the red pen got through maybe half her stack while I was there. The guys at the counter laughed at something and the seriousness broke and then came back. Marcus said goodbye when I left and used my name and I don't know when he learned it or how long he's known it, whether it was month one or month seven, whether I've just been someone who comes in and doesn't fully arrive until today, on a Monday, waiting for an email, finally looking up long enough to see the room I was in.

Maybe that's what familiar places do sometimes. Wait until you're tired of whatever you're carrying and then show you they've been there the whole time.

entries.prompt: The email is still out there waiting to land. But set that aside for a minute — what's something completely unrelated that's been quietly taking up space in your head this week? Walk me through the last time it surfaced: what you were doing, what pulled your attention there, where your mind went.
entries.content:
The thing that's been in my head is embarrassing to write down, but here it is: I've been thinking about the chair.

The chair at my desk at home. The one I bought secondhand off an app the week I moved here, paid forty dollars for it, carried it up three flights of stairs with the help of exactly zero other people, and have been sitting in for almost two years. It's bad. It's always been bad. The left armrest has a specific give in it, this soft lateral wobble, and if I sit in it for more than an hour my lower back starts doing a thing that I've just incorporated into my relationship with having a lower back. I've replaced the dying plant's soil, I've upgraded my coffee setup twice, I've bought a second lamp because the overhead light was making the evenings feel like an interrogation room. I have not replaced the chair.

It surfaced Thursday night. I was sitting in it at the desk, laptop open, halfway doing something for work and halfway just being in the chair being uncomfortable, and I shifted to try to find the position that's less bad than the other positions and the armrest did the wobble and I thought: I could just replace this. I could go online right now and buy a chair that costs a hundred and fifty dollars and solve this problem that I have been voluntarily living inside for twenty-two months.

And then I didn't. I shifted again, found the slightly-less-bad position, and kept reading the thing I was reading.

I've been thinking about why, on and off, since Thursday. It's a chair. The analysis I'm about to do is disproportionate to the object. I'm aware of that. I'm doing it anyway because it's 10pm and the email still hasn't come and I need somewhere to put my head that isn't the email.

Here's what I think it is, or part of it: the chair is from the first week.
