The forty-dollar chair is a piece of the person who moved to this city not knowing how long they'd stay, who bought furniture at the level of commitment implied by forty dollars because buying a real chair would mean deciding to be here, actually here, putting-money-into-here here. And I've been here almost two years and I still have the chair, which means some part of me has been treating this as temporary since the day I arrived. Not consciously. But the chair is the evidence.

I said something to Avery once, not recently, early on, where I called the apartment *my place* in a way that was slightly performatively casual, like I was signaling that I knew it wasn't permanent and she shouldn't worry about the implications of that. She didn't say anything about it. At the time I thought that meant she'd received the signal and we were fine. Now I think it's possible she just didn't engage with the performance because she's better at ignoring the performance than I am at executing it.

The job thing. The maybe-different-city. I've been telling myself it's separate from the chair but I don't think it's separate from the chair. I think the chair is the same conversation as the job, just furniture-shaped. The conversation being: how long are you going to keep treating where you are as a waiting room for somewhere you'll eventually decide to actually be.

I looked at chairs online for about twenty minutes on Friday morning. There's one that's under two hundred dollars and has reasonable reviews and comes in a color that would look fine in the corner. I added it to my cart. It's still in my cart. I've looked at it twice since then and not bought it.

*It's a chair. This is insane.*

I know. But also: is it insane. Or is the chair exactly the right size for the thing it's actually about.

Avery stayed over Thursday. She was there when I was doing the chair-wobble and the not-buying-it, though she was in the other room and didn't witness any of it. When she left Friday morning she said she'd see me this weekend and kissed me in the specific way that's more greeting than farewell, the kind that means *see you soon* rather than *goodbye,* and I stood in the open doorway for a second after she left and watched her go down the hall and thought about the chair and thought about the email and thought about November and thought about this apartment that I've been living in like someone who isn't sure they've decided to live in it yet.

The chair would be fine. Buying it would not change anything about the job or the city or the maybe-different-city. It would just mean my back didn't hurt.

I brought it up to Avery this afternoon in the kind of offhand way that I know by now is me testing whether something feels as strange out loud as it does in my head. I said: I think I need to replace my desk chair, I've been putting it off for two years for no reason. She said: yeah, you should, that thing looks like it's trying to hurt you. I said: I added one to my cart and then didn't buy it. She looked at me for a second, the slightly fond exasperated look, and said: is this about the chair.

I said: mostly.

She said: okay.

And let it sit there. Didn't push. Didn't finish the sentence I hadn't quite started. Just looked at me and let me be in the middle of something without requiring me to resolve it in front of her.

I'm going to buy the chair. This week, maybe tomorrow, before I find a new reason to put it off. Not because it solves anything, not because it means I've decided anything, but because the lower back thing is real and the forty-dollar chair has done its job and the job was never supposed to be permanent. It was supposed to get me through until I was ready to be somewhere. And I've been ready for a while. I just keep acting like I haven't noticed.

The email or no email. November or after November. Whatever the shape of the next thing is.

In the meantime: chair. Arriving in three to five business days, which is how long it takes for an object to confirm that you live somewhere.

entries.prompt: The chair is still broken. Walk me through what actually happens the moment you decide not to buy the new one — what do you tell yourself, and where does the thought go from there?
entries.content:
The cart has been open in a tab since Friday.

I looked at it again this morning, on the bus, which is a new low — scrolling past reviews of ergonomic foam and adjustable lumbar support while the city does its thing outside the window, not watching the city, watching a chair I'm not buying. The reviews are all four stars. There are two one-star reviews and I read both of them in full even though they're about shipping damage and have nothing to do with whether the chair is a good chair. I read them and then I closed the tab and put my phone in my pocket and looked out the window for the last four minutes of the ride.

Here's what I tell myself: I should wait until I know more about the job situation.

That's the story. That's the one I ran this morning when I had the tab open and the moment arrived where I could have just bought it — card number memorized, address saved, three to five business days, done — and instead I closed the tab. I said: wait until you know. It's not a good reason to wait, a chair purchase does not require labor market information, but it arrived fast and sounded reasonable enough that I let it do its work and moved on.

The thought went to the email, which is where every thought goes eventually, the way water finds the low point. Hasn't come. Nine days since the meeting and the room where I leaned forward before I'd approved the movement, and nothing. I've been refreshing less, like I said, twice instead of eleven times, but the two times have a quality to them. A held breath. The brief hot feeling in my face before the inbox confirms it's still just newsletters and a thing from my building about a package.

And from the email the thought went to the city question, which is where it always goes after the email, the specific shape of the not-knowing. Somewhere that's not here. Hybrid, but. The office is not here. And the here is Avery's particular way of handing me coffee, and the creak of the specific floorboard, and the forty-minute farmer's market, and November and the porch, and a coffee shop where Marcus knows my name and I've apparently been invisible to myself for eight months. The here is the whole thing I've been building without calling it building.

So: don't buy the chair until you know what city you're buying a chair for.

That's the logic. Laid flat it sounds almost rational for about four seconds and then it sounds like the conference room. *I've got room for it.* The nod I needed. The yes before the question finished. I've been doing that operation my whole life, apparently — offering things up before I've thought them through, saying words that sound like decisions while my actual decision-making is somewhere else entirely, running a face that knows what it's doing while the inside is still doing the math.

Only this time it's the inverse. This time I'm withholding instead of committing, and the logic is the same underneath. Both are about not-deciding. Both are about keeping the moment where it is a little longer so I don't have to find out what comes next.

*The chair is a chair. You can buy a chair in any city. They sell chairs everywhere. This is not the question.*

I know that. I know that.

On the bus I thought about the apartment and what it would mean to actually be in it. Not in the waiting-room way, not the forty-dollar way. Just — be here, the way you're supposed to be somewhere you live. Avery knows the creak of my floorboard too, by now. She said once, not recently, that she could tell my mood from how fast I was moving through the apartment, the specific rhythm of it, whether I was carrying something or just walking. I didn't know I had a rhythm. But she knows it. That's its own kind of accumulated evidence and I keep not filing it.

The email might come tomorrow. Or next week. Or it came and went and I'm waiting for a reply that's not arriving because the reply was a different rejection, quieter, the kind that's just absence. I've been trying to hold all three versions at the same weight and mostly managing it, except at two in the morning when weight distribution becomes less precise.

I texted Nat this afternoon. Not about the chair, just — texted her. Asked what she was doing this week, said November felt close, said I was glad we figured out the calling. She sent back a voice memo that was forty-seven seconds, which is brief for Nat, that just said: *me too, honestly, I've been thinking about the porch, I think we're going to talk for twelve hours straight and need the whole next week to recover, which is exactly correct, see you soon, also what's going on with the job thing, call me.* 

I'm going to call her tonight. Not about the job thing specifically, or maybe the job thing, but mostly because it's a Tuesday and she picked up on the third ring the last time and I've been getting better at doing the thing when the thought arrives instead of putting it in the pile.

The chair is in the cart.

I opened the tab again just now, at the kitchen table, with intention this time. Looked at the four-star rating, the photo of it in three color options, the reasonable price, the three-to-five business days. Thought about my lower back, which is doing the thing right now, the familiar ache from the armrest that's been doing the wobble since March of last year.

Avery would say: *is this about the chair.* And I'd have to say: mostly, and the mostly would be smaller tonight than it was yesterday. The job is the job. The city question is the city question. The chair is a chair that I've been sitting in for twenty-two months while I waited to be ready to be somewhere, and I've been ready for a while, and tonight I'm buying the chair.

I bought the chair.

Confirmation in my inbox, right next to the newsletter, right next to the space where the other email still isn't. Three to five business days. Color: the one that'll look fine in the corner.

The chair in the corner will be evidence. I'll sit in it and my back won't hurt and it'll be a thing I own in a place I live.

That's all. That's the whole thing. It doesn't solve the job or the city or the email or any of the stuff that's still in the middle of being in the middle. But I'm done waiting to decide I live somewhere.

The old chair I'll leave on the curb. Someone will take it. Someone always takes things from the curb, there's a kind of faith in that, the city's quiet agreement that nothing goes to waste if you leave it in the right place.

entries.prompt: What's something you own that you've been meaning to get rid of — not because it's broken, but because keeping it around means something you haven't quite named yet? Walk me through the last time you noticed it.
entries.content:
The old chair is on the curb.

I dragged it out this morning before I could think about it too long. Down three flights, the left armrest doing its wobble the whole way, and then onto the sidewalk with a small cardboard sign that said FREE in marker because I didn't want it to just sit there looking abandoned. Stood there for a second looking at it from the outside, maybe for the first time. It looked worse than I'd registered. The seat had a depression in it, a permanent Kai-shaped indent, twenty-two months of sitting there in the not-quite-decided mode and apparently the chair remembered every day of it. Someone's going to take it, I know they will, the city has that agreement. But while I was standing there I had a second of almost wanting to bring it back upstairs, and I want to write about that before I stop having it.

The new chair comes Thursday. Three to five business days and Thursday is day four so it's probably Thursday. I have three days of the old chair being gone and nothing in its place, which means I'm working from the kitchen table tonight, back against a chair that's actually worse than the desk chair but in a different way, more acute, less chronic.

The wanting-to-bring-it-back thing. I know what it was. The chair is the obvious thing — I already wrote that whole entry, I already bought the replacement, I already did the part where I admitted it was about more than the chair. What I didn't write was that the old chair also has a box of stuff under it.

Had. Had a box of stuff under it. The box is still upstairs.

I left the chair. Brought the box back inside. Put it on the desk where the chair used to be and then relocated it to the closet because I didn't want to look at it.

It's a banker's box, standard issue, the kind with the lid that doesn't quite fit right. I got it from the supply cabinet at work, I think, or maybe I brought it from college, I don't remember. Inside it there are: a hoodie from a 5K I ran with Ben and Nat and Priya junior year that I haven't worn in maybe three years but also haven't thrown away; a mug that's technically fine but that I never use because I have better mugs now; some cables for devices I no longer own; a birthday card from my mom with a long message that I've read maybe once since she gave it to me; and a photograph, printed out and printed badly, that's been in there since I moved and that I have looked at exactly twice.

The photograph is from graduation night. All four of us, Nat's camera with the self-timer, one of those photos where everyone's slightly wrong — someone's eyes are half-closed, someone's hand is at a weird angle — but the thing in the middle of it, the specific quality of being twenty-two and together and not yet scattered, that came through anyway. I am mid-laugh in it. I have no idea what anyone said to make me laugh like that but I'm laughing without reviewing it first, without managing what my face is doing, just a person being caught in a feeling.

I noticed the box last Thursday, actually, when I was trying to find something in the closet and it was blocking what I was looking for. I moved it, found the thing, shoved the box back without looking at it. Then Friday I was sitting in the bad chair and I thought about it, which is the real answer to the prompt, which is: I notice the box when I'm thinking about the in-between. The not-quite-decided-yet. The version of me that moved here with forty-dollar furniture and treated the whole thing as provisional. The box is the other forty-dollar chair, the thing I brought here from before that I've been keeping in case it turns out to be relevant again. In case I need evidence of who I was.

*Why would you need evidence of who you were.*

I don't know. I think I've been afraid that if I throw away the hoodie and the mug and the bad cables and maybe especially the photograph, I'll be confirming that the thing it represents is also gone. The four of us in the photo. The not-yet-scattered. The laugh without reviewing. If the box is gone then I have to admit the box was always just a box and it can't give back anything it was holding for me.

But November is two weeks away.

That's the thing. That's the thought I was having tonight when I dragged the chair out and came back upstairs and opened the closet and stood in front of the box and decided to leave it there for today. In two weeks I'm going to be on a porch with the actual people from the photograph and we're going to find out what the gap is, and I've been writing about that with this mix of anticipatory grief and wanting and something that feels like readiness, and the box is sitting in my closet like a hedge. Like a just-in-case. Like some part of me that's still not sure November will give me back the thing I've been storing proof of.

What I think is actually true, which I'm writing down flat so I have to see it: November is not giving anything back. That's not what November is. November is not the photograph, it's not the 5K hoodie, it's not the four of us at twenty-two on a timer with someone's hand at a weird angle. November is Nat asking the question that goes underneath and Ben saying something that makes me laugh harder than I expected and Priya talking for eleven minutes straight about something that is somehow also about everything, and all of us being thirty seconds away from needing to learn each other slightly again, and doing it. That's different from the photograph. That's better, probably, or at least more real, and I've been treating the box like proof of the thing that November is supposed to replace instead of just. A box.

I'm not throwing it out tonight.

But I'm going to go through it before November. Not as a ritual, not as a project. Just actually decide what's in there and what it's doing. The cables can go. The mug can go to the Goodwill box I have in the corner. The birthday card I'm going to actually read, the whole long message my mom wrote, which I've been avoiding in the way you avoid things that will make you feel something specific. The hoodie I might keep, not under the desk but in the actual closet with the actual clothes, because it was a good day, we walked a 5K in October when it was cold and then ate too much and argued about something and none of it mattered and I want to be able to wear a sweatshirt without it being an act of preservation.

The photograph I'll move somewhere I can see it. Not as evidence. Just because I like the version of myself mid-laugh, the unreviewed one, and I want him somewhere visible while I'm figuring out how to make him less of a special occasion.

Three to five business days. Thursday. The new chair comes and I sit in it and my back stops doing the thing.

The old chair is on the curb. I walked past it tonight on the way back from getting groceries and someone had already taken the sign, just the sign, the chair was still there but the sign was gone, which seems like a very specific kind of bad omen that I'm choosing not to think about.

It'll be gone by morning. It always works that way.

entries.prompt: The chair is gone and something else is taking up the space it left — not furniture, but a feeling you haven't quite named. Walk me through what the last few days have actually felt like without it there.
entries.content:
The chair is gone. The new one came Thursday, assembling instructions and everything, which took forty minutes and a YouTube video because one of the bolts was stripped and I had to work around it in a way that felt like a metaphor I'm refusing to accept. It's good. It's actually good. The lumbar thing works, the armrest doesn't wobble, I sat in it for three hours Thursday night and my back didn't do the thing. That's a solved problem. I solved it. Forty dollars to two hundred dollars and twenty-two months and the problem is solved.

So why does the corner feel weird.

That's the thing I've been sitting with. Not literally sitting with — I've been sitting in the new chair, which is fine, which is correct — but carrying around since Thursday in the background, underneath the bus and the coffee shop and the work thing and the email that still hasn't come. The corner where the old chair was looks the same. The new chair is in it. The box is out of the closet and I went through it like I said I would — cables in the trash, mug in the Goodwill bag, birthday card read in full, which I'll come back to — and the hoodie is in the actual closet with the actual clothes and the photograph is on the desk propped against the wall where I can see it from the new chair. Everything is where it should be. Nothing is worse. The corner doesn't feel wrong exactly. It feels like when a sound stops and you suddenly notice you'd been hearing it the whole time.

The birthday card. My mom wrote three paragraphs. I've been avoiding it since she gave it to me because the handwriting alone, the specific slant of it, does something to me that I don't have a clean word for. The message was — she talked about being proud in the particular way my mom is proud, which is adjacent to worried, always adjacent to worried, the pride and the worry arriving together in the same sentence so you can't fully receive one without the other landing too. She said she knew the first year would be hard and she hoped it was getting easier. She said she thought about me more than I probably realized. She said she loved me and was proud of me and then added a P.S. that was about making sure I was eating enough protein, which is so completely her that I sat there for a minute just breathing.

She wrote that at graduation. That was two years ago. Two years of the card sitting in a box under a bad chair in a corner of an apartment I was treating as temporary, and she was thinking about me more than I probably realized, and I have been too busy doing laps to call her back as much as I should.

I called her Friday. Not because of the card specifically, or not only. I'd been meaning to more, I've known that, it sits in the same pile as the Ben call and the Priya call — the thing I kept not doing because I'd built it up into a thing to manage. The calls with my parents where I spend the first twenty minutes fielding questions and then manufacture reassurance and then hang up feeling wrung out. I've been telling myself that story about those calls for two years and it's not entirely wrong but it's also not entirely right, and I think I've been using the wrung-out part to avoid the other part, the part that is just my mom who was worried about my first year and hoped it was getting easier.

It was getting easier. I didn't tell her that in any real way at the time. So Friday I called and I told her it had gotten easier and then I told her some of it — not the whole texture, not the operations center, not eight months of managing my face in an elevator — but enough. The checkpoint, which she knew about vaguely. Avery, more than I'd said before. The November trip. She got quiet when I mentioned Avery, the specific quiet that is her trying not to make a thing of something she wants to make a thing of, and I said: it's good, Mom, it's actually good, and she said: I can tell, you sound different. I asked different how. She said: more like yourself.

More like yourself.

I've been turning that one over. Ben said *more settled* and it bothered me for months. My mom said *more like yourself* and it did something else, something that sat lower. Because settled I can argue with — settled is surface, settled is the maintained shape, settled doesn't mean anything about what's happening inside. But *more like yourself* assumes she knows what myself is. And the thing is she does. She knew the mid-laugh person in the photograph. She knew the one who made things happen, who booked the thing, who called first. I've been thinking of that person as a past version, someone I've been trying to reconstruct, but my mom heard him on the phone on a Friday afternoon and said there you are, basically. There you are.

I don't know how to hold that without feeling the thing about the card sitting in the box for two years. The protein P.S. She was worried about me eating enough protein and I was treating the card as something to deal with later and later kept extending until later became now, and now I'm on the phone and she's saying there you are.

The email still hasn't come. Eleven days. I checked twice this morning, once on the bus and once when I got in, and there's a quality to the checking now that's different from the first week — less like holding my breath and more like checking the weather. Something I do and then put down. The not-knowing has stopped feeling like a wall I'm standing against and started feeling more like weather I'm in. Cold, maybe, a little uncomfortable, but the kind of thing that ends on its own schedule regardless of what I do about it.

Avery came over Saturday. I showed her the new chair, which she sat in and declared structurally sound in a tone that made me feel slightly ridiculous for having mentioned it as a thing. She saw the photograph on the desk and picked it up without asking, looked at it for a second, and said: *is that Nat?* I said yeah, and Ben, and Priya, taken junior year, graduation night. She looked at it a moment longer and then set it back down against the wall. Didn't say anything else about it. But later, on the couch, she said: I'm glad you're going. November. I said yeah, me too. She said: you seem like you need it and I don't mean that badly. I said I knew she didn't.

I need it. That's right. That's the exact right word and she said it without me having to work up to it.

The corner holds the new chair and the new chair holds me and my back doesn't hurt. The box is gone from under the desk and the hoodie is in the closet and the card is on the bookshelf now, not hidden, just there. The photograph is on the desk propped where I can see the mid-laugh when I sit down to work.

There's space where the thing that was temporary used to be.

I'm filling it, I think. Slowly. Without always knowing I'm doing it.

entries.prompt: The birthday card — you mentioned reading it in full and then moved on. What was in it, and what did you do with it after?
entries.content:
My mom has terrible handwriting. Not illegible exactly, more like every letter is making a private decision about which direction it wants to go. I've been reading it my whole life so I can decode it, but you'd need a key. She knows it's bad, she's always known, she signs birthday cards with a small apology at the bottom sometimes — *sorry for the handwriting, you know how it is* — except this one, this card, she didn't apologize. She just wrote.

Three paragraphs, plus the P.S.

First paragraph was the pride-adjacent-to-worry one, the one I described already, where the two feelings arrive together in the same sentence so there's no clean way to take the compliment without also receiving the concern. She said she knew moving somewhere new alone was hard in ways you couldn't prepare for. She said she hoped I'd found at least one person to get a meal with. That sentence — one person, a meal — she set the bar low on purpose and I understand why and I still felt it. She wasn't sure I had anyone. She was giving me permission to have almost no one. She was being gentle about something she was scared of.

Second paragraph was about the job. She said she didn't fully understand what I'd be doing but she trusted that I did, and if I ever stopped understanding it I should remember that every job looks like noise for the first year and then it stops or it doesn't, and either outcome is information. She said my dad had spent six months thinking he'd made a mistake at his first real job and then one day it clicked and he stayed fifteen years. She said: *you don't have to love it in the first year, you just have to let the first year be what it is.* 

I didn't let the first year be what it was. I know that. I spent the first year performing someone who was managing the first year rather than just being in it, which is a distinction I couldn't have articulated until recently. She was already telling me to do the right thing and I read the card once and put it in the box.

Third paragraph is the one I've been sitting with since Friday.

She wrote: *I know you think we worry too much. You're right. We worry because when you were little you took things harder than you showed, and we learned to watch for what you weren't saying, and then you grew up and moved four states away and we lost our angle on it. That's not a complaint. That's just what it feels like from here. We're still watching, just from farther away and with less information, and we want you to know that whatever the thing is that you're not saying, you can say it. We're not going anywhere.*

I had to put the card down when I got to that part.

*We learned to watch for what you weren't saying.* She'd known. Since I was little. The whole operation I've been running — the managed voice, the smooth version, the eight minutes to sound effortless — she'd been watching that happen in real time when I was eight years old and she'd just been waiting, from four states away, with less information.

And then: *we're not going anywhere.*

Same words as Avery. Almost exactly. Different context, different person, different relationship, same words. Like there's been a group chat I didn't know about where the people who know me have been comparing notes and landing on the same sentence. *We're not going anywhere. I'm not going anywhere. I know. We know.* All of them, apparently, standing outside a door I kept thinking I had to hold shut for everyone's protection.

I sat with the card for a while after I finished it. Not crying exactly but adjacent to it, the specific pressure behind the eyes when something true arrives and you weren't ready. Then I put it on the bookshelf. Not in the box. Somewhere visible.

I've been thinking about what to do with the third paragraph. Not the card — the card's staying, I'm not putting it away again — but what she said. *The thing you're not saying.* She was talking about the calls, about the distance, about the first year, about all of it in general. But she was also, without knowing the specifics, describing the exact thing I've been writing about for months. The checkpoint, the alarm, the Tuesday at 11pm, the ceiling at 10:30, the email still sitting in its absence. She was describing the whole landscape from four states away with less information and she got it right.

I told her some of this on Friday. Not the way I'm writing it now, not that organized, more like — things coming out in the order they were ready to come out. I told her about Avery and I could hear her trying not to make a thing of it and mostly succeeding. I told her about the trip, November, the porch. She said: go, that's exactly right, go. She asked about work in the way she always asks, slightly braced for bad news, and I said it was okay, some of it was actually good, I'd figured something out about a project and it had landed. She said she was glad. And then she said, kind of quietly, almost past it: *are you letting people in a little more?*

I said yeah, I think so, I'm working on it.

She said: *that's all it is. That's the whole thing.*

Like it was simple. My mom, who I've been keeping at phone-call distance for two years because the calls were too much, said *that's the whole thing* like she'd been waiting to say it.

The P.S. was about protein. Specifically about getting enough protein because she'd read something about how people in their twenties don't eat enough protein and she'd been thinking about it. She listed three easy sources, with options. It's possible that's exactly the right thing to put at the end of a card like that, something so her, so specifically and undefendably her, that you don't end on the weight of the third paragraph. You end on: have you tried Greek yogurt.

I've been eating more Greek yogurt. That's not about her. I just thought it was time.

November is in ten days. The photograph is on my desk where I can see the mid-laugh. The card is on the shelf. The new chair holds me and my back doesn't hurt.

I'm going to call her again this weekend. Not for a reason. Not a scheduled check-in I've been manufacturing reassurance for. Just because she said *that's the whole thing* and I want her to know she was right, even if I just say it in the form of calling on a random Saturday when nothing particular happened. She'll probably ask about protein. I'll probably let her.

That seems right. That seems like exactly the right way to tell someone you're not going anywhere either.

entries.prompt: Your mom set the bar low on purpose in that card — *one person, a meal* — and you understood why, and it still landed. What did you actually want her to say instead? Walk me through the version of that paragraph you were half-hoping for.
entries.content:
She wanted me to have one person. One person to get a meal with. She underlined it by not underlining it, just let it sit there in the sentence like she'd thought about it and decided that was the ask, the minimum viable condition for her son to be okay four states away. One person. A meal.

I understood why she did it. I understood it the second I read it, which is probably why I put the card in the box and didn't take it out for two years. She was scared and she was being gentle about being scared and she didn't want to say *I hope you're not eating alone every night for months* so instead she said *one person, a meal* and trusted me to hear the rest of it underneath.

I heard the rest of it. I've been hearing it for two years without knowing I was hearing it.

Here's the version I wanted. Not a comfortable version to write but I've been at this long enough that I know what comfortable means in this context and it means approximately nothing. So:

I wanted her to say she knew I'd find them. Not hoped. Not prayed, which is a thing she sometimes does in a way I don't fully share but also can't argue with. *Knew.* The specific verb of certainty, the one that says I've watched you long enough to understand what you do when you arrive somewhere new, and you find your people, and it takes you longer than it should because you're slow to trust the good thing, but you get there. I wanted her to say: *you're going to find people who matter.* Not *I hope you'll find at least one person to get a meal with* — that's the scared version, the pulled-punch version. The one I wanted was the version where she'd actually looked at who I am and decided she wasn't worried.

But she was worried. She was allowed to be worried. She learned to watch for what I wasn't saying when I was eight years old. She had sixteen years of watching before I moved away and then nothing, just phone calls where I manufacture reassurance and she knows I'm manufacturing it and we both pretend she doesn't know. Of course she set the bar low. She was protecting herself too.

The other thing I wanted, and this is harder to say flat: I wanted her to say she trusted me with it. The new place, the new job, the whole thing. Not *I trust that you know what you're doing* — she said that in the second paragraph and I was grateful but that's about the job, about competence, about whether I'd made a reasonable choice. What I wanted was something underneath that, something like: *I trust you with yourself.* I trust you to notice when something's wrong and to do something about it before it gets worse. I trust you to ask for what you need. I trust you to let someone in, eventually, in your particular slow careful way.

She didn't say that. She said we'd lost our angle on it and we were watching from farther away with less information. Which is honest and I love her for the honesty and it's also the sentence that says: we're not sure. We've seen how you hold things and we're not sure you'll ask for help before you need it.

I think she was right to be not sure. I had been in this city for almost a year when she wrote that card and I had an expensive bad chair and a dying plant and a job I didn't fully understand and I had been eating pad thai out of the container over my keyboard more nights than not. I had not found one person to get a meal with in any meaningful sense. I was managing the shape of being fine rather than being fine, and she knew it from four states away with less information, and she set the bar at one person because she thought that was what I could do.

The thing is — the thing that got me, that made me put the card down for a minute — is that she was also wrong. Not about what I was doing. About what I was capable of. She saw the scared version and calibrated to it and the calibration was accurate to the data and the data wasn't the whole picture. There was always going to be Avery, the coffee and the creak of the floorboard and the *I'm not going anywhere* in the dark. There was always going to be Ben, who I called eventually, who said *definitely* in the voice that meant he also meant it for himself and I heard both meanings. There was always going to be November and the porch and Nat asking the question that goes underneath.

I don't think she could have known that. I'm not sure I could have known it. But I wanted her to have known it. I wanted the card to say: *I know it's going to take you a minute, and I know you'll make it harder than it needs to be, and I know you'll manage the shape of things for longer than you should — and then you'll do the thing. You'll find the people. You'll say the true thing eventually. You'll let someone in.* I wanted her to have had enough faith in me to skip past the worried version and go straight to the true one.

She set the bar at one person because she loved me and she was scared. The version I wanted set the bar at whatever was actually possible because she loved me and she was sure.

The difference between those two things is small and it's everything.

I'm going to call her this weekend. I said that last entry and I meant it and I'm writing it again so it stays somewhere outside my head. And when I call I'm going to tell her she was right about the whole thing, the letting people in, the that's all it is. And I might also tell her she was selling me a little short. Not in a way that's a complaint, not in a way that makes her feel bad — she did exactly what she should have done from four states away with less information and I put the card in a box for two years so I can't talk. But maybe I'll say: you hoped I'd find one person and I want you to know that was too small a hope. Not because I'm impressive or together or any of the things I've been performing. Just because the people were there. They were always going to be there. Even when I was being an idiot about letting them.

One person to get a meal with.

Mom. I found four. And one of them hands me coffee without turning around because she knows from the sound of the floorboard that I'm there.

That's the paragraph I wanted.

entries.prompt:
The last few entries have stayed close to your mom and what you wanted her to say. Time to go somewhere else.

The birthday card thread has run deep — and before that, the chair, the box, the email. All of it has been pretty solitary. What hasn't surfaced in a while: a moment with another person that didn't go the way you expected — not dramatically, just slightly off. The Mar 27 prompt touched performing-vs-feeling, but that was weeks ago and came from a different angle.

---

Think about the last time you left a conversation feeling like something went unsaid — not a fight, not a falling out, just a moment where the exchange ended and you carried something away with you that the other person probably didn't know you were carrying. Walk me through the conversation: what was said, what wasn't, and where your head went on the way home.
entries.content:
The November trip is in four days and I've been in a good stretch — the kind where you don't examine it too closely because examining it is how you end it — and then Tuesday happened and now I'm on the bus home carrying something the other person definitely doesn't know about.

It was with Reese. That's the thing. Not a Drew interaction, not Avery, not anyone I've been writing about in ways that would make the feeling make obvious sense. Reese, who I've graduated from coworker to something slightly warmer, who brings food back for the team and asks the specific question instead of the general one, who I almost told about the callback before I caught myself doing the wrong version of the thing. Reese, who I've been sharing the bad kitchen coffee with two or three mornings a week for months now.

We were in the elevator at the end of the day and Reese asked, offhand, how things were going. Not work things. The how are you version. And I said good, actually good, which was true and not performed, just true. I mentioned November in passing — the trip, the porch, the friends I haven't seen in a year. And Reese said something I wasn't expecting, said it in a voice that was easy and casual and completely sincere: *that sounds really nice. I miss having that. Friends who go back.*

And I said yeah, it's going to be great, and the elevator opened and we went our separate directions.

That was the whole thing. Thirty seconds, maybe less. Nothing happened. No fight, no moment of friction. And then I was outside on the sidewalk heading for the bus and I had this feeling, this specific low uncomfortable thing, that I'd done it wrong. That Reese had said something real and I'd bounced off it with *yeah, it's going to be great* and kept moving.

I miss having that. Friends who go back.

I've been turning that sentence over on the bus and I'm still turning it over now, sitting at the kitchen table, and here's the thing I keep snagging on: I heard it. I heard what was underneath it. Reese has been at this job almost two years, started six months before me, and I have never once thought about what Reese's evenings look like, whether Reese has the group chat, whether there's a Nat or a Ben or a Priya on the other end of a call. I've been thinking about Reese as the person in my peripheral vision, the competent one, the clean-structure one, the one I ran eight minutes of Slack messages to seem unaffected by. I've been thinking of Reese as something that happens to me rather than a person who has a whole story going on independently.

*I miss having that.*

Past tense. Miss. Not *I don't have close friends* or *I'm lonely* — Reese wouldn't say that in an elevator, no one would, that's not the elevator register. But the sentence was there and it said something real and I received it and said *yeah, it's going to be great* and walked out into the cold.

What I could have said. I keep running this. Something like: *do you have people from before, from wherever you were?* Something specific, something that acknowledged I'd heard the thing she was actually saying. I didn't need to make it a whole conversation right there in the elevator bay with our coats on. Just a question. Just the thing that says: I heard you, you're not just scenery.

I didn't. The habit is old and the elevator opened at the right moment and I had the November feeling in my chest and I was warm from it and I just let the exchange end.

On the bus I thought about the coffee shop. Marcus knowing my name, the woman with the red pen, the two guys at the counter. I spent eight months looking at my phone in a room full of people having a morning and then one day I left my headphones on the counter and found a room full of people. And Reese has been twelve feet from my desk for almost two years and I've been treating the relationship like something that happened in my peripheral vision, contextual, a data point in my ongoing assessment of whether I was measuring up. Not a person who rides an elevator at 6pm and says something true in the key of casual.

I feel bad about it. That's the simple version. I feel bad about all the times the exchange ended and I'd gotten what I needed from it — validation, comparison, the specific relief of a coworker approving of my work — without ever asking what Reese needed from it. I feel bad in the way you feel bad when you realize you've been treating someone like furniture in your own story.

But here's the thing I'm trying not to clean up, the uncomfortable one: I'm not sure I would have known how to ask. Even if the elevator had stayed closed another full minute, I'm not sure I had the muscle for it yet. The Nat call, the Ben calls, the Priya call, the ceiling at 10:30 — all of it has been about people I already loved, people I had prior claim on, people whose patience I'd already received enough evidence of. Reaching toward Reese would be something different. An extension, a beginning, something that doesn't have an old shorthand to fall back on if it goes sideways. And in the elevator at 6pm I didn't have any of that available and so I said *yeah, it's going to be great* and moved.

I don't think Reese went home and felt anything about it. That's probably right. Thirty seconds, an offhand comment, nobody's keeping score on a Tuesday elevator exchange. The thing I'm carrying is mine. Reese is fine.

But I've been thinking about Wednesday morning. Whether I bring it up — not the elevator specifically, not *I've been thinking about what you said*, that would be strange, that would make it into something it wasn't. More like just. Asking. Returning the question with some actual interest behind it. *How are you doing* without the work in front of it, without the performance underneath. See where it goes.

The bad kitchen coffee tastes the same regardless of what you put into the conversation. That's a fixed variable.

November is in four days. I've been so inside the going — the porch, the friends, the finding-out-what-the-gap-is — that I forgot other people have their own version of what I've been circling. The group-chat thinning, the friends scattered, the wanting to have a person who goes back. Reese said it in twelve words in an elevator and I walked past it and I've been on the bus ever since thinking about how easy it would have been to just stop for a second and ask.

Tomorrow morning, bad coffee, bad light.

I'll ask the specific question.

entries.prompt: Reese said something you weren't expecting — and the entry cut off before you got there. What did Reese actually say, and what happened in you the moment you heard it?
entries.content:
Wednesday. The bad kitchen coffee, the bad overhead light, Reese at the counter rinsing a mug.

I got there at the same time, which happens maybe twice a week, this coincidence of needing coffee at 9:40. I poured mine. Reese was doing the thing with the mug. And I thought about Tuesday's elevator the whole bus ride in, thought about *I'll ask the specific question* like I'd made a decision, like the deciding was the hard part.

Then we were both standing there and I said: hey, the thing you said yesterday — about friends who go back. I was thinking about it.

Reese turned around with the mug. Not surprised exactly, more like — the expression of someone who said something real on an elevator and then filed it under probably nothing and now has to recalibrate.

*Oh*, Reese said. *I didn't mean to make it weird.*

I said: you didn't. I just wanted to — I heard it, is all. Do you have people from before? Somewhere?

And Reese looked at the mug for a second. Then said: yeah. But they're all at home, which is Seattle, and I moved here right out of school for this job because it was the job, you know, and I thought I'd go back in two years. That was three years ago. And it's fine, it's my life, I like my life here, but there's this thing where you're always a flight away from the people who knew you when you were a person who didn't know how to do any of this yet.

That's the sentence. That's the one I've been carrying since 9:43 this morning.

*The people who knew you when you were a person who didn't know how to do any of this yet.*

I stood there with my bad coffee and thought: I have been sitting twelve feet from this person for almost two years and that sentence was in there the whole time. While I was running eight minutes of Slack message about a document and running math on whether my structures were as clean as Reese's structures, Reese was three thousand miles from Seattle and the people who knew the before version.

I said: I almost didn't come here. The job, I mean. I almost took something closer to home.

Reese said: why didn't you?

I said: I thought this one meant more. And I didn't know anyone here, which felt like a reason not to come and then felt like a reason to come, and then I was here and for a while I kept waiting for it to feel like a mistake and it didn't exactly, it just felt like a different thing than I expected.

Reese said: yeah.

Just that. *Yeah.* The specific yeah that means I know that thing and I'm not going to make you explain it in a kitchen at 9:43 in the morning.

We stood there for a second. The light was doing what it does, the overhead hum. Someone walked through and nodded at both of us and kept going.

I don't know what I thought would happen when I asked the specific question. Maybe something neat, a beginning, a clean exchange. What actually happened was weirder and better: Reese said a true thing and I said a true thing and we stood in the light that makes everyone look slightly unwell and it was just two people who happened to end up in the same city for work and have been making bad coffee next to each other for twenty months and apparently both had the before version and the after version and hadn't had occasion to mention it.

Then Reese said: Seattle people are good. We talk. It's just different from being there, which you know.

I said I did know. November, I said. This weekend. I'm going to see mine.

Reese said: that's good. Tell me how it goes.

Not politely. Specifically. The same way Ben says *let me know* — like a tab being left open.

I said I would. And then we both went back to our desks.

On the way back down the hall I kept replaying *the people who knew you when you were a person who didn't know how to do any of this yet.* The thing it did when I heard it. Not the Reese content exactly, not even the Seattle details — something underneath, the structure of the sentence, the logic of it. There's a version of you that existed before you learned how to perform having figured it out. Reese has those people, in Seattle, three thousand miles away, the same way I have mine scattered across four time zones, the same way I've been writing about the thinning and the trip and the fear that the gap would be bigger than I could stand.

What I felt when I heard it — I'm trying to be precise, I'm trying not to clean it up: I felt something like recognition that didn't have anywhere to put itself. Recognition of someone else's specific thing and then this sudden strange awareness that I hadn't looked up long enough to see it was there. Two years of the peripheral vision, the measurement, the *Reese's structures are cleaner*, and in the kitchen in the bad light I found out we were in the same situation from different angles, and the measurement had nothing to do with it, and I'd wasted a lot of mornings on the wrong reading of the room.

Not wasted. That's dramatic. The mornings happened. The document got done. The checkpoint cleared. I got there.

But there was something available that I'd been walking past, is what I mean. Something I could have had earlier if I'd asked the specific question earlier. Same as the phone call with Ben. Same as the Tuesday night with the tea. Same as every time I've been circling the real thing and then doing one more lap.

The lap-running is getting shorter, though. That's what I keep noticing. Last year the Ben call didn't happen for six weeks. The Avery ceiling thing took eight months. The Priya call was almost a month of *I'll get to it.* The Reese thing was one day. Thirty seconds in an elevator and then seventeen hours and then I walked into the kitchen and asked before the coffee was poured.

That's something. That's a real thing that happened in a direction I can point to.

November in three days. The porch. The fireplace. Nat asking the question underneath and Ben saying the thing that makes me laugh harder than I expected and Priya talking for twelve straight minutes in a way that is somehow always about everything. All of them knowing the version of me that existed before I knew how to do any of this.

I want to come back from November and tell Reese how it went. Not the performed version, not the *it was great, so good to see everyone.* The actual version. The gap and what it turned out to be. Reese will probably ask *did it feel different than you expected?* and I'll probably say yes and mean it.

The tab Reese left open. I'm going to use it.

Four days. The porch is waiting.

entries.prompt: The November trip is four days away. Walk me through what you're actually imagining when you picture arriving — the specific moment you get there, who's around, what it feels like to be in that room with those people after a year.
entries.content:
Four days.

I keep trying not to picture it too specifically because every time I do something tightens in my chest that I can't tell is excitement or dread or the specific cocktail of both that shows up whenever something I've been wanting for a long time gets close enough to be real. The trip has been theoretical for so long. The group chat, the *we should really*, the plans that dissolved back into links and voice memos. And now there's a confirmation email and a porch with two wooden chairs in the photo and I leave Friday morning and I can't stop running the arrival scene even though I know the arrival scene is not the point.

Here's what I actually picture.

Ben gets there first. I don't know why I'm sure of this, I just am — Ben has always been the first one somewhere, he shows up before the thing starts in a way that's not anxious, just how he moves through the world. He'll have already figured out where everything is, the kitchen, the firewood, which bedroom has the best light, and he'll have opinions about all of it that he'll share without being asked. I picture him opening the door when I knock, or maybe the door is already open, and the first thing he does is something physical — a hand on my shoulder, one of those back-of-the-neck grabs, the greeting that's too quick to be managed. And I'll have a bag in my hand and the cold air from outside and I'll be standing there in someone else's house that we all rented together and it'll be — I don't know. I don't know what word goes there.

Then Nat arrives and it starts.

That's the image that keeps arriving. Nat coming in, her bag, that specific energy of Nat entering a room, which is not loud but is present in a way that fills the space up to the edges. She'll say something to me immediately, something that picks up mid-sentence, like we've been in the same conversation for the last year and she's just rejoining it. That's how Nat works. The months become irrelevant in the first thirty seconds. She'll look at me for one actual second — not performance, just a read, the Nat read, the one that goes underneath — and then she'll move into the house and it'll be fine, we'll all be fine, and I'll feel the thing I've been afraid wouldn't come back and it'll be back.

Priya is the variable. Priya will either be early or an hour late with a story about why she's an hour late that is somehow worth the hour. That's not a complaint. That's love. When Priya finally gets there it'll be the four of us and something will close that's been open for a year and I won't know I'm feeling it close until I notice my shoulders have dropped.

That's the arrival. That's the version I keep running.

But here's what I'm also picturing, the thing underneath the warm version: the first two hours. Before we find the rhythm. Because I've been writing about the gap, the fact that we've been becoming people separately and not watching each other do it, and the gap is real, and the first two hours is when you find out the actual size of it. There will be a moment, I'm almost certain, where someone makes a reference that used to land and it half-lands, where the timing is slightly off, where I say something and read it as it goes out and think: *that's the newer version, that's the one they don't know yet.* And that moment is going to be a little like the thing my mom described, watching for what's not being said. I'm going to be watching all of us for what's not being said. Old habit.

What I'm hoping is that the moment is small. That the gap is smaller than the worried version I've been carrying for months. That the shorthand comes back fast and the two hours feels like nothing and then we're on the porch in the cold with drinks and the easy stupid thing has found us again, the slide, the thing after the real thing.

What I'm actually okay with, if the gap is bigger — is that we do what you do with a bigger gap. We introduce each other to the people we've become. Nat meets the version of me who said a true thing to his manager and then said a true thing to the ceiling at 10:30 and then bought a chair instead of treating this city like a waiting room. I meet the version of Nat I've been getting glimpses of on the phone, the one who asks the question underneath and waits, who said *everyone could see it but you* like it was fond and not a verdict. We compare notes on who we've been becoming and it takes some work and that's okay. That's just what it is now.

I've been thinking about the photograph. Graduation night, the timer, all four of us slightly wrong. I'm mid-laugh, unreviewd, caught. I moved it to my desk so I could see it from the new chair, and I've been looking at it every morning when I sit down, not with nostalgia exactly, more like — checking in. Seeing if I recognize the person. And lately I think I recognize him more than I did in January, more than I did six months ago, which is not because I've gone back to being twenty-two but because I've gotten better at laughing without reviewing the laugh first. Or at least the gap between the laugh and the review is getting smaller. Same as the lap-running.

November is the test of that, maybe. The real one. Not the checkpoint with Drew, not the overdone pasta conversation, not the 9:43 kitchen with Reese — those were practice in single dimensions. November is four people in the same room and all of it at once and no prep I can do that will help once Nat looks at me and I look back and we find out whether the thing that was always there is still there.

I think it's still there. I've thought that for a year while also being afraid it wasn't. Writing this now, four days out, I think it is.

Ben will have found where the firewood goes. Priya will be in the middle of a story about the drive. Nat will look at me with the look and I'll look back and we'll know. And then someone will say something stupid and we'll all be off and the porch will be cold and the fireplace will be going and we'll talk for twelve hours and need the whole week to recover and that'll be exactly right.

Four days.

The plant has seven leaves and I'm leaving it in Avery's care, which I told her and she said she'd keep it alive, which I believe completely. She knows what she's doing with living things.

I said goodbye to her this morning before work and she said: *tell them I said hi.* Then she thought about it and said: *actually tell them I said I know why they matter to you.* 

I stood in the doorway for a second. Then I said okay. Then I went to the bus.

Four days.

entries.prompt: The trip is four days away and Ben is already vivid in your imagination — but think back further: when did you two actually become the kind of friends you are now? Walk me through the moment, or the stretch of time, when something shifted.
entries.content:
Four days out and I can't sleep and I've been lying here thinking about Ben, specifically, and I know why. I wrote him into the arrival scene as the first one there, the door already open, the hand on the shoulder — and when I was writing it I realized I knew exactly where the image came from. Not a fantasy. A memory. There's a specific moment, or not a moment but a stretch of a few weeks, where I can locate the shift. Before that stretch we were friends in the group-of-friends way, orbiting the same people, ending up in the same rooms. After it we were the other thing.

Sophomore year. January, so cold the radiators in the dorm were doing this desperate clanking, and I was going through something with a class I wasn't going to pass if I didn't figure out how to ask for help, and I didn't know how to ask for help, and I'd been carrying it in the managed way for about six weeks, telling everyone it was fine, running the version. I don't even remember the class now, I remember the feeling of it — the specific weight of knowing something is going wrong and performing knowing it's about to go right.

Ben figured it out, I think, from the outside. Not from me saying anything. He just — noticed. The way he noticing works is quiet, you don't catch it until after, you just suddenly realize he's been tracking something you thought you were hiding.

We were in the dining hall and he said, not looking at me, over whatever he was eating: *what's the class you're avoiding.*

Not a question. A statement with the inflection of a question, which is different, which means he already knew the answer and was giving me the door.

I said nothing for a second. And then I said the class.

He said: okay. When's the next thing due?

I told him. He said come over Tuesday, we'll work on it. Not *I'll help you,* not *do you want help,* not any of the framings that would have let me say no thanks, I'm fine. Just: *come over Tuesday.* Like it was already decided and I was just being informed.

I almost didn't go. I spent Monday running the math on whether showing up would mean admitting I needed the thing, which it obviously would, which is why I was considering not going. Classic. But I went. I knocked on his door on a Tuesday in January with snow coming down outside and he opened it and said *okay, show me what you have* before I'd taken my coat off.

We worked for three hours. He didn't solve it for me — that's the specific thing I keep returning to — he just kept asking the question one layer down from where I'd gotten stuck, and then when I got there he'd ask the layer below that. It was not fun, it was kind of miserable actually, my brain resisting the whole time the way my brain does when it's afraid of finding out what's there. And Ben sat on his floor with a textbook he didn't need for any of his classes and kept asking the layer-down question like he had all the time in the world.

Near the end, when I'd gotten through most of it, he said: *why didn't you say something three weeks ago.*

I said I thought I had it.

He said: you clearly didn't have it.

I said I know that now.

He said: *I know you knew that then.*

And there it was. Not mean — just accurate. The thing someone says when they're not buying the performed version and also not making a big deal of not buying it, just calling it by its name and moving on. I remember my face going somewhere, some involuntary recognition, and then he said: *next time just say.* And opened his mini-fridge and handed me something and we talked about something else for an hour.

Next time just say. Four words. Said like it was easy, like all you had to do was do it.

That's the moment I mean when I say there was a shift. Nothing dramatic. No big conversation, no revealed truth, no milestone I can point to and say: that's when. It's more like: something happened in that room that made me understand Ben was a person I didn't have to perform at. That the managed version was not required. That showing up with the thing still broken was an acceptable arrival condition.

We never talked about it explicitly after that. We didn't debrief the Tuesday or assign it meaning. We just kept showing up in each other's lives with slightly less distance than before, and the slightly-less accumulated into the thing we have now, the February call, the *definitely* in the voice that meant something, the tab left open. I think that's how it works with people. Not a declared moment but a thing that proves itself over time, the evidence accumulating until one day you're in a dining hall in January and you say *the class I'm avoiding* and the other person says *okay, when's the next thing due* and the decision has already been made, you just have to show up Tuesday.

I've been thinking about what I'm showing up with this weekend. Not a problem exactly, not a broken thing. More like: the whole year. The accumulation of it. The checkpoint and the alarm and the ceiling at 10:30 and the chair and the card. Avery saying *more here* and Reese saying *when you were a person who didn't know how to do any of this yet* and Drew saying *you got there.* The plant with seven leaves that was dying when I first started paying attention to it.

I want to put all of that in the room with them. Not as a presentation, not as the version I've been workshopping, just — let it be in the room with us. Let Ben see what the year actually looked like. Let Nat ask the question underneath and actually have somewhere to take the answer this time. Let Priya talk for eleven minutes about something adjacent to it until she lands on the exact right word from an unexpected angle because that's what Priya does.

Let myself be known by the people who went all the way back to the Tuesday in January when I didn't have it and showed up anyway.

That's what I'm arriving with. That's what I'm bringing through the door that Ben will probably have already found open.

Next time just say.

I'm getting better at that. I've been practicing.

Three days.

entries.prompt: The Ben entry cut off — you were describing the moment he figured something out about you from the outside, without you saying anything. What did he actually do, and what did it feel like to be seen like that before you'd asked to be?
entries.content:
Three days.

I've been lying here since midnight and the question keeps arriving and I can't do anything with it except write it down.

What did it feel like to be seen before you'd asked to be.

The honest answer is that it felt like being caught. Not punished — caught, the way you catch something falling before it hits the ground. The grab happens before the thing decides to fall and before you've asked anyone to catch it and before you've even fully admitted to yourself that it's falling. And there's this half-second where you're aware of the grab and you don't know whether to be relieved or angry about it or both.

I've written about Ben noticing from the outside, but I don't think I've been specific about what the noticing actually looked like, how it arrived. I keep going back to the dining hall, the statement with the inflection of a question, *what's the class you're avoiding* — but that was already the result of something, that was Ben having already done the looking. The actual moment of being seen came earlier and I witnessed it happen to me in real time and did not know that was what was happening.

We were in someone's room, five or six of us, the usual configuration — people on the floor, someone's desk chair pulled out, the general low-level chaos of a Tuesday night that wasn't supposed to turn into a whole thing and did. I was present in the way I was present that whole stretch of January, which is to say physically there and internally elsewhere, running the situation with the class on a background loop. Laughing at the right moments, tracking the conversation, doing the version. And at some point — I don't even remember what was being said — Ben looked over at me. Not a long look. Not pointed. The kind of look someone gives when they're triangulating something. And then he looked away and kept talking to whoever he was talking to.

That was it. That was the whole visible thing.

But I felt it. I felt the look in some way that was underneath the social surface, some register I didn't have a name for. Not self-conscious, not the hot feeling of being observed doing something wrong. More like — the specific sensation of someone's attention landing accurately. Like a key that fits. I don't know how else to say it. He wasn't looking at the version I was showing the room. He was looking at something else, something I hadn't offered for looking at, and the look was brief enough that I could pretend I hadn't noticed, which I did, immediately and completely.

And then a week later: *what's the class you're avoiding.*

The thing about being seen before you've asked is that it removes a choice you've been very carefully maintaining. I had a whole operation built around that choice — whether to show the thing, when, to whom, in what form, with what amount of preemptive management. And the operation assumes the other person is waiting for permission to see. That's the deal, that's the implicit contract. I don't show until I'm ready. You don't look until I show.

Ben just — didn't do that. Not rudely, not in a way that made me feel exposed in a bad way. He looked at the thing and then waited a week and handed me the door in a dining hall in January and didn't make me explain how he already knew.

What it felt like. I've been trying to get this right for the last hour.

It felt like relief I was angry at.

The relief was immediate and involuntary, the same kind as the shoulder-drop I'm picturing happening on the porch when Priya finally arrives and the four of us are all there. Something unclenching. Oh, I don't have to hold this alone. Oh, someone already knows. Oh, Tuesday is fine because he already knows and the knowing isn't catastrophic, the room didn't end, nothing I was afraid of happened.

And underneath the relief: this low irritated feeling, almost like grief, at how long I'd been holding the thing alone when I didn't have to be. That's the part that doesn't get to be comfortable. All the laps I'd been running, all the managing, and Ben had seen through the managing from some ordinary Tuesday vantage point and it cost him one look and five words and then an evening on a dorm room floor asking the layer-down question. And I'd spent six weeks performing having it together to everyone including him, six weeks of extra weight I was carrying that he could have helped me put down if I'd been able to say *I don't have this.*

Next time just say. He said that at the end. Not as a lecture, not as a lesson, just as the most obvious thing in the world, stated plainly and then dropped. Next time just say. As if the only thing standing between me and being helped was the act of saying I needed it, as if that were a small and simple act, as if the whole operation I'd been running wasn't specifically designed to make that act feel impossible.

*I know you knew that then.* He said that too. I've been returning to that sentence more than the other one, lately. Because he wasn't telling me he'd seen something I'd hidden. He was telling me he'd seen that I was hiding it on purpose, which is a different accusation, and he made it without making it an accusation at all, just another piece of information, just the thing that was true.

He saw that I was doing it. Not just that I was struggling — that I was struggling and choosing to show the version instead. And he didn't confront it, didn't make me explain it, didn't ask me why I do that or whether I know I do that or how long I've been doing it. He just named it, once, in that flat way of his, and then handed me something from the mini-fridge and we moved on.

I think that's the thing I've been trying to understand for three years, writing now in the dark three days before the porch. Not the relief. The part after the relief. The part where someone sees the hiding and doesn't treat the hiding as the main event. Where the thing you've been protecting gets acknowledged and then just — set down, gently, and you're allowed to move on without having to justify how long you were holding it.

I've been doing that with Avery lately. Not perfectly, not even most of the time. But sometimes. The ceiling at 10:30. The pasta that got overdone. The *I applied for something at 11pm and I didn't tell you.* Saying the thing and then finding out the room didn't end and moving on.

I learned to do that in a dorm room in January with snow outside and a mini-fridge and a textbook Ben didn't need. He taught it by doing it, not talking about it. By seeing me before I asked to be seen and making it the most ordinary thing in the world.

Three days. He's going to open the door. He'll have already found where everything is.

I'm going to walk in and not perform it.

That's the whole plan.

entries.prompt:
The last few entries have lived inside the Ben thread — and before that, the trip, the arrival scene, being seen. Time to surface somewhere else.

What's something you got unexpectedly good at during a stretch of your life you wouldn't otherwise want to go back to? Walk me through what that time actually looked like, and how you figured out the skill was something that had stayed with you.
entries.content:
Two days before the porch and I can't stop thinking about the winter I got good at being alone.

Not the philosophical version, not the whole *learning to be with yourself* framing that sounds like something you'd put on a mug. The actual skill. The specific competence of filling a Saturday that has no plan in it and nobody coming over and making it not terrible, not by performing contentment but by actually finding the shape of a day that's only yours. I got good at that the first winter here. The one I wouldn't go back to.

I know I've mentioned it in parts — the takeout containers, the bad overhead light, the dying plant that was evidence of something. But I don't think I've said what it was actually like to be in it, the texture of the days. It was quiet in a way that had weight. The apartment was new in the way rented places are new, which is to say it held no memory of me yet, every surface still waiting to find out who I was. I didn't know anyone well enough to call without a reason. I kept inventing reasons. The city outside the window was full of people who had histories in it and I was a person with a job and a bus route and exactly one barista whose name I knew.

Weekends were the hardest thing. Weekdays had structure, had the bus, had Drew's opinion to want and work deliverables to perform competence at. Weekends were just the apartment and me and however many hours until Monday gave me somewhere to be. I spent a lot of them on my phone, doing the thing where you scroll without reading because you need the sensation of information even when there's no information you want. I ate bad food over the counter. I called my parents more than I wanted to and less than they deserved. I went to the coffee shop sometimes just to be in a room with other people, put my headphones in, and then sat there feeling the specific loneliness of being surrounded and still alone.

Somewhere around month three I started taking walks that weren't going anywhere.

That's where the skill came from, I think. I'd leave the apartment with no destination because no destination was available and I'd just walk. The city teaches you things when you stop trying to use it to get somewhere. I found the street with the good bookshop smell coming through the grate, I found the block where someone had a window garden going even in November, I found the corner where the light comes off the building at 4pm in a way that doesn't make sense geometrically and I've never figured out the physics of it but it's good and I started routing past it on purpose. None of this was strategy. It was just what you do when the apartment is too quiet and you've already eaten the thing in the fridge and your thumbs are tired from scrolling.

What I was learning, without knowing I was learning it, was how to be the only observer of a thing. No one to show it to. No second opinion. The light off the building was the light off the building and I was the only person who knew I'd seen it and it was still worth seeing. That sounds small. It was not small. I'd been running the performance operation for so long that I'd started to think things only mattered if someone else was there to confirm they mattered. The first winter here slowly convinced me otherwise, not through any revelation, just through repetition. Good thing. No audience. Still good.

The walking taught me to eat alone in restaurants, which I'd been afraid of in a way I hadn't admitted. Not the actuality of it, the idea of what other people would think, the story I assumed strangers were writing about the guy at the two-top by himself. Turns out strangers are not writing that story. Strangers are eating their own food and thinking about their own things. I started eating alone at real restaurants on Saturdays, sometimes with a book I didn't always read, sometimes with nothing at all, and the world did not produce commentary on my solo status and the food was good and I was there and that was a complete experience. That competence is still with me. Avery will sometimes want her own evening and I'll go somewhere good and eat by myself and be fine, actually fine, not performed fine.

The other thing I got good at was making a decision about a day without it being endorsed. Deciding: today I'm going to the museum, just me, and then going, and not needing anyone to say that sounds like a good plan. Deciding: today I'm staying in and I'm going to read the thing I've been meaning to read and make coffee twice and not feel like I'm failing at having a life. The first winter generated this capacity I didn't know I was missing, which is the ability to be my own authority on whether a day was worth having. I spent the whole first year running days by an imaginary panel in my head — would this look okay from the outside, is this what someone settled and functional does on a weekend — and the winter slowly dissolved that panel by giving me no alternative. There was no outside. It was just me and the apartment and the walk and the light off the building at 4pm. And eventually the panel stopped convening and I stopped needing it to.

I'm not saying I'd go back. I wouldn't. The weight of that quiet was real and I don't miss it. But the thing it gave me has stayed. I can feel it when I'm on the bus without my headphones now, when I sit in the coffee shop and actually look up, when I take the long way to work because the long way is better in October. Some version of me learned to be present in a room even when the room was just myself, and that version got to practice it all winter with no witnesses, and now — I don't know. Now there are witnesses and the being-present has somewhere to go. Avery, the porch, Wednesday morning in the bad kitchen light. The skill is the same skill. It just has more to do with it now.

I'm going to pack tomorrow. The house-with-the-porch requires something warmer than what I've been wearing, I checked the forecast twice and it's going to be genuinely cold, and somewhere in the apartment there's a sweatshirt that used to live in a box under a bad chair and now lives in the closet with the actual clothes. I'm going to bring that one. Not as a symbol. Just because it's warm and it fits and I've been wearing it again, which it turns out is the whole point of owning something.

Two days.

entries.prompt: The porch is two days away. But set that aside — think about someone you've been a worse friend to than you'd like to admit, not because anything blew up, but just because you let distance accumulate without really deciding to. Walk me through the last time you were in contact with them: what was said, what you thought afterward, and where things stand now.
entries.content:
Priya.

It's always been Priya, and I've been writing around it for months.

The last real contact was the voice memo she sent in October. Twenty-three minutes, which is short for Priya. A story about a coworker I've never met, a thing about her apartment building's laundry room situation that escalated in ways that were somehow both mundane and genuinely insane, and then, at the end, almost like she'd been building toward it the whole time: *I don't know, I've just been thinking about everyone lately, I miss us, I miss when we could just be in the same room, okay I have to go, love you, talk soon.*
