I sent back four minutes. I laughed at the laundry room thing. I said I missed them too, said November was going to be good, said I was glad we were doing the trip. Four minutes, decent energy, no long pauses where I ran out of real things to say. Good voice memo. Completely fine.

What I thought afterward: *that's enough. That counts. We're good.*

What I actually know, sitting here two days before I see her for the first time in a year: it wasn't enough. It counted in the sense that a thing happened, words were exchanged, the friendship registered as active. But Priya had said something real in the last forty seconds of twenty-three minutes — *I miss when we could just be in the same room* — and I'd sent back four minutes of surface and called it reciprocal.

The thing about Priya, the thing I've been knowing and not saying, is that she's the one I've been most careful with. Not because she's fragile, she's not fragile, Priya is one of the most functional people I know in the sense of actually moving through her life and dealing with things as they arrive. But because something about the way Priya processes — out loud, in real time, fully, with follow-up — something about that has always made me slightly want to manage my intake. She'll ask how you are and she'll actually mean it and she'll wait for the answer and then she'll have thoughts about the answer and those thoughts will be accurate in ways that catch you off guard, and somewhere in the first year here I started rationing how much I gave her to respond to.

Not consciously. I didn't decide. But I can see it now in the length of my voice memos back to her. Hers: eighteen minutes, twenty-three minutes, one that was thirty-one. Mine: four minutes, six minutes, once eleven and I remember that one feeling like I'd overextended. She never said anything about the ratio. Priya wouldn't. She'd just keep sending the long ones and trust that the short ones back meant I was still there.

I was still there. I was just giving her the version with less material.

I've been doing that with everyone, I know that, it's been the whole subject of these entries for months — the managed voice, the constructed person, the alarm. But with Nat I've been getting better. With Ben I've been getting better. The calls, the asking the specific question, the being seen before I'd asked to be. With Priya I've just been — maintaining. Sending the four-minute voice memos that say I'm still there without saying what being there actually looks like.

And the reason is stupid and embarrassing and I'm going to write it down anyway: Priya was the one who saw me hardest in college, in the specific uncomfortable way, the way where you can't fully enjoy being seen because being seen means being known and being known means losing the right to be unknown if you decide later you'd rather go back to that. She has this ability to describe what's happening with someone and be accurate and not ask for permission first. Not cruel. Just — clear. I've been a little afraid of her clarity. I've been feeding her things that are true but small enough that the clarity has limited material to work with.

*She's been listening to your voice memos more than she's said anything about.* She told me that. And then I put off calling her for most of a month and then called and had a decent conversation that was still, I think, missing something. And two days before I see her I'm realizing the something was: I never gave her the full version. I gave her enough to be reassured that I was okay, not enough to show her what okay actually looked like from the inside.

I want to fix that this weekend. Not as an agenda, not as a plan I execute when I arrive. But I want to actually tell Priya what the year was like. Not the summary, not *it was a lot but I think I found my footing* — the texture. The conference room and the nod. Avery and the ceiling and the alarm. The box under the chair. The photograph. I want to let her be accurate about it. I want to hand her the material and see what she does with it instead of keeping her at the curated distance where she can tell I'm fine but can't tell what fine actually cost.

Here's the other thing. The thing I've let accumulate alongside the distance: I don't know what's actually going on with Priya. Not in a bad way — she seems good, she seems like herself, the voice memos are long and warm and full of life. But I've been so inside my own managed maintenance that I haven't asked the specific question. Not once in eight months. I've been receiving what she sends and responding and not asking what's in the parts she doesn't send, because asking would mean opening the door to being asked back, and I wasn't ready for that until recently.

I'm ready now. Or closer to ready than I've been.

The thing that's strange about Priya, and I don't mean this badly, is that she would have been fine with less. She's not someone who keeps score, who tallies the imbalance and brings a grievance. She sends the long ones because that's how she is, not as a demand. The distance that accumulated between us accumulated in my direction, by my management, and she stayed steady and warm on her end and trusted that I'd find my way back when I was ready, which is almost worse than if she'd noticed and said something because it means she's been carrying more generosity toward me than I've been bringing toward her.

I'm going to tell her that. Not as a confession, not as a thing that needs resolving. Just — I know I've been sending short voice memos and I know why and I'm here now and I want to actually talk. That's enough. That's what *next time just say* looks like in this direction.

Two days.

The porch is waiting. Nat with the question underneath, Ben with the door already open, Priya with twenty-three minutes of material and patience and the specific clear-eyed Priya readiness to describe what's actually happening with someone as soon as they let her see enough to do it.

I'm going to let her see enough.

I've been practicing.

entries.prompt: The trip is behind you now. Walk me through the moment it was actually over — the goodbye, the drive home, whatever the last scene was — and what you carried back with you that you didn't have when you left.
entries.content:
Sunday night. The apartment is quiet in the way it's only quiet after I've been somewhere loud for a few days — not peaceful, not yet, more like the kind of quiet that still has voices in it if I don't pay attention.

The last scene was Priya's car pulling out of the gravel driveway at 11am. I was standing on the porch with my bag and Ben was next to me and we watched her go, and she had the window down even though it was genuinely cold, and she was saying something through the window as she pulled away, finishing a sentence she'd started inside, and she was still talking as the car turned and then she was gone. That's so completely Priya that I want to leave it exactly there.

Then it was me and Ben, which is how it started too — he'd arrived first, door already open, exactly as I'd pictured it, which was both confirming and slightly surreal, like the world briefly agreeing to cooperate with a plan I'd made in my head. And standing on the porch at the end with him felt like the bookend I hadn't known I needed. He said: *good trip.* I said yeah. He said: *next time don't wait a year.* Not mean. Just fact. I said okay. He punched my shoulder — not hard, just the contact, the hand-on-shoulder I'd pictured in the arrival scene — and then he loaded his bag and drove away and I stood there for maybe two full minutes in the cold before I went in to get my stuff and close up the house.

The drive home was four hours and I didn't listen to anything. That's new. A year ago I would have had a podcast on before I got out of the driveway, something to fill the space where thoughts live. This time I just drove. Let the weekend settle.

What I carried back.

The gap was smaller than I'd been afraid of. That's the first thing. Not gone — I want to be honest about that, I don't want to make the weekend into a reconciliation narrative that's cleaner than it was. There were moments in the first few hours that had exactly the slightly-off timing I'd prepared for. References that half-landed. A stretch on Friday afternoon where we were all talking and I caught myself monitoring, watching for what wasn't being said, doing the old operation. And then somewhere around Friday dinner the monitoring stopped and we were just in it. I didn't notice the switch until later, when I was lying in the dark in the too-cold bedroom and thinking about the evening and realized I couldn't identify the last time I'd been aware of performing anything.

Nat asked the question underneath. Of course she did. We were on the porch, second night, fire going inside but we'd gone out anyway because the cold felt right, and she said: *so what was the year actually like.* Not *how was the year*, not *how are you.* The actual question. I told her. Not the whole essay, not all the entries in sequence, but the real shape of it — the checkpoint, the alarm, the ceiling at 10:30, the box under the chair, the email that came Tuesday while I was gone, which I'll get to. She listened and didn't fill the silences and at the end she said: *you sound like someone who finally moved in.* 

I said yeah. I think that's right.

The email. I didn't check it the whole trip, which I'd told myself I wouldn't do, which I actually managed, and then when I was in the car on the way home I felt the pull of it, the old static, and I pulled over at a rest stop and opened my phone.

They went with someone else. That's what it said, in the professional language that means *no*, framed carefully as *we were very impressed but ultimately another candidate's experience was a closer match.* I sat in the parking lot at the rest stop and waited to find out what I felt.

What I felt was: okay.

Not performed okay, not the okay that's managing something underneath. Just — okay. The door turned out to be a wall after all, or maybe it was a door and I didn't need to go through it right now, and either way I'm here, in the city I've been treating like a waiting room and finally started living in, with the new chair and the seven-leaved plant and the person who hands me coffee without turning around because she knows from the floorboard. The finding-out happened, which is what I said I wanted, and what I found out was that the wanting was never really about the job. It was about needing to know there were other hallways. Now I know. The knowing is enough.

I texted Avery from the rest stop: *coming home. Couple hours. I want to tell you about the weekend.* She said: *I'm here.* Then she said: *the plant is fine, seven leaves, don't worry.* Then she sent a photo of the plant on the windowsill with too much afternoon sun coming in behind it, a little blown out, the leaves visible.

I looked at that photo for a while before I got back on the road.

What I carried back that I didn't have when I left. I'm trying to be exact about this.

I have the thing I knew was in the gap — that we're still us, that the shorthand came back, that the slide is still available, the easy stupid thing after the real thing. That's not nothing. That's the main thing. I was afraid of finding out and I found out and it's okay, the group is okay, the thinning is real and manageable and we can close some of it if we do the actual thing instead of the group-chat approximation of the thing.

But what I didn't expect: Priya. Two nights on a porch in the cold and I gave her the material, the real version, and she was accurate about it in exactly the way I'd been rationing against, and the accuracy didn't hurt the way I'd been preparing for it to hurt. She said: *you've been doing the same thing the whole time you've been describing doing it differently, which means you're doing it differently.* Said it with the Priya directness that I'd been managing my intake of for eight months. And I just — laughed. Because she wasn't wrong, and the not-being-wrong felt like the thing Ben's look felt like in the dining hall sophomore year. Someone accurate, someone landing correctly, someone who wasn't waiting for permission to see.

I said: I know. I'm still figuring it out.

She said: *I know you know. That's the thing.*

I'm carrying that home. *I know you know.* The thing that's available when you stop feeding someone the curated version and let them use the good material.

The apartment smells the same. It always smells the same when I've been gone a few days, like it was just being itself without me in it, no audience, continuing. I walked in and put my bag down and stood in the kitchen and it felt like the kitchen of someone who lives here.

Avery came over late, after I'd had an hour to settle. She sat on the new chair, which made me laugh, and I sat on the couch, and I told her about the weekend and the email and the rest stop and the photo she sent of the plant. She listened the way she listens, all the way to the end. When I was done she said: *how do you feel.* I said: like I moved in.

She smiled in the specific quiet way.

I think that was the right answer. I think that was the whole year's answer, finally arriving on the right night in the right kitchen.

Seven leaves. All holding.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been explaining to yourself one way for a long time — and when did you last catch yourself wondering if that explanation was actually true? Walk me through what happened the last time the doubt surfaced.
entries.content:
The story I've been telling is that I left home because the job was better. That's it. That's the version. Drew's company, this city, the bus route and the bad chair and the corner coffee shop where Marcus knows my name — all of it traceable back to a single clean decision: better opportunity, had to take it. I've told that story enough times that I stopped hearing it as a story. It became biography.

The doubt surfaced Tuesday. I was in the coffee shop, no headphones, doing the thing I do now where I actually look up. The woman with the red pen was there, which is ordinary now, she's just the woman who is always there grading things. Someone new was at the counter, older, looked like he was meeting someone who hadn't arrived yet, kept checking the door. And I was sitting with my coffee cooling and not thinking about anything specific and then I was thinking about the job I didn't get. The email at the rest stop. *Another candidate's experience was a closer match.* I've been fine about that, genuinely fine, the okay-at-the-rest-stop feeling has stayed okay. But Tuesday I was sitting there and I caught myself running a different question than the one I'd been running, which was not *do I wish I'd gotten it* but *why did I actually apply.*

I applied because I was in the hallway. I've said this. That's the version.

But here's the part I kept circling on Tuesday, in the coffee shop, with the woman grading her papers and the man watching the door: I applied to a job in a different city eight months into the most real thing I've had since college. That's not nothing. That's a specific timing. I didn't apply in month three when the apartment still didn't know me yet and I was eating over the counter and the plant was dying. I applied after the Saturday mornings and the coffee and the ceiling at 10:30. After *I'm not going anywhere.* After I'd finally started treating this place like somewhere I actually lived.

*Why then.*

I've been telling myself: hallway feeling, needed proof of other doors, the checkpoint anxiety made everything feel trapped. All true. I believe all of that. But Tuesday I was looking at the man watching the door for whoever hadn't arrived yet and I thought: what if the timing wasn't incidental.

What if some part of me applied because things were getting real.

Not a flattering thought. I sat with it for a while in the coffee shop and it didn't become less uncomfortable with sitting. Because here's the shape of it if I pull it all the way out: I spent eight months learning to want things out loud, learning to not redirect before the sentence was done, learning to let the evidence mean what the evidence means. And then right in the middle of all that learning, right when it was actually working — I sent an application to somewhere else at 11pm on a Tuesday.

That could be coincidence. Probably is, mostly. The checkpoint anxiety was real. The hallway was real. But the version I've been running, *I needed to know there were other options,* that version never asked why I specifically needed to know that specifically then. And I think the honest answer is: because staying was starting to feel like a choice I was actually making, and a choice you're actually making has stakes in a way that a place you've been treating as provisional doesn't.

The forty-dollar chair. The box under it. Two years of treating this as temporary so I didn't have to be here on purpose. And then I started being here on purpose and something in me got scared and sent a resume somewhere at 11pm.

*That's dramatic. You applied for a job. People apply for jobs.*

I know. I know that.

But I've been in a stretch of catching myself and I want to catch this one too, while it's still catchable, before the story calcifies into the version that makes me look like someone who was just being sensibly exploratory rather than someone who got close to having something real and then looked sideways for an exit that he wasn't even sure he wanted.

I told Avery last night. Not all of it, not the full analysis, but the doubt. I said: I've been thinking about why I applied when I did and I'm not sure the reason I've been using is the whole reason.

She was quiet for a second. Then she said: what do you think the other part is.

I said: I think I might have been scared. Of this. Of staying on purpose.

She didn't say I know this time. She said: *yeah. I wondered.*

Which means she'd been carrying that read for months, while I was performing the *I just needed to know there were options* version, and she'd held it and not said anything until I got there myself. Which is — I don't know what to do with that exactly, the patience of it, the specific long-term generosity of someone who sees the thing and waits for you to arrive at it rather than walking you to it by the sleeve.

*I wondered.* She handed me my own truth back like it was the most ordinary thing.

I said: I'm sorry if that was — I don't know. Disorienting.

She said: it was a little. But you came back.

And something in the chest did the thing, the arrival thing, the heavy-thing-finally-put-down thing. *You came back.* Not a verdict. Just a fact. I applied somewhere else and I got the no and I came back and the coming back was its own kind of choosing, maybe cleaner than the choosing I'd been avoiding when I sent the application in the first place.

I think the story isn't wrong exactly. The hallway, the needing-other-options — that's in there, that happened. But it's not the whole story. The whole story includes: I got close to something I wanted and I looked for an exit and the exit didn't materialize and when it didn't I pulled over at a rest stop and felt okay about it and then drove back to the city and texted the person who hands me coffee without turning around.

Maybe that's the version I should have been telling. Not the clean single explanation. The full one, with the scared part visible.

The man in the coffee shop — whoever he was waiting for eventually arrived. I looked up from my not-quite-phone, not-quite-anywhere state and they were hugging, the specific relieved hug of someone who thought maybe they'd been stood up and then hadn't been. The whole room got slightly warmer for about four seconds.

Then they sat down and ordered and the room went back to being a room.

I finished my coffee and took the long way to work. The light off the building at 4pm is going to be different today because it's November now and the angle changed, but I'm going to walk past it anyway to find out what different looks like.

entries.prompt: What's something you said out loud recently that sounded right in the moment — but you heard yourself saying it and thought, *that's not actually what I mean*? Walk me through the conversation: what you said, what you meant, and where the gap came from.
entries.content:
Monday. Reese and I were both in the elevator after a long one, bags on, coats already on, and Reese said: *so are you staying or what?* Not an interrogation, just the way Reese asks things, direct and casual at the same time, like the asking is just a formality and she's comfortable with whatever the answer is.

She was asking about the job. The email. She knew I'd been waiting on it because I'd told her I would, after the 9:43 kitchen conversation, after she'd left the tab open. She'd been tracking it the way she tracks things, quietly, without making a thing of it.

And I said: *yeah, I'm staying. It just wasn't the right fit.*

It came out easy. Clean. The performed effortlessness, the thing I built in eight minutes and sent in thirty seconds. Reese nodded, said that made sense, said something about how these things go, and we walked out into the cold together and split at the corner.

I heard myself say it on the way out. *Wasn't the right fit.* Like I'd done an evaluation and arrived at a conclusion. Like the decision was mine and I'd made it from a position of clarity and sufficiency rather than — the rest of it. The 11pm application. The specific scared timing. The rest stop and the okay that was real but also wasn't the whole thing.

The gap came from wanting to look like someone who had handled a thing rather than someone who is still in the middle of figuring out why they did it.

Which is the old operation. I know that. I knew it in the elevator, actually, I heard the words leave and felt the slight flatness behind them, the particular texture of saying-something-true-but-incomplete in a voice that implies completeness. *Wasn't the right fit* is not a lie. It probably wasn't, practically, in all the ways that can be evaluated. But it's not what I meant. What I meant is something more like: I applied because I got scared of staying on purpose and then the exit didn't appear and somewhere at a rest stop in November I found out the staying was what I actually wanted all along. That's what I meant. That's the version with the real material in it.

I could have said it. Reese has the receipt from the 9:43 conversation, from *the people who knew you before you knew how to do any of this yet.* Reese is someone who said a true thing in an elevator and let me do something with it. I could have said: actually, I think I applied because things were getting real and I panicked a little, and the no turned out to be fine, and I'm glad I'm here. That version is also only twenty seconds long. It doesn't require anything the other version doesn't require.

What stopped me, if I'm going to be specific about it: the coming-back feels private still. *You came back.* Avery's sentence. The thing she said last week that did the arrival thing in my chest. That exchange is mine, it belongs to a specific person and a specific kitchen, and I think what I was protecting in the elevator wasn't the image of myself as someone competent and settled — or not only that — but something more like the real story, which I haven't figured out how to hold in a casual after-work conversation without it becoming more weight than the moment calls for.

That might be true. That might also be the old excuse with a new coat on.

I've been sitting with it all day. The difference between protecting something real and just defaulting to the managed version — I haven't always been able to feel the difference. I'm getting better at it. But Monday in the elevator it came out before I could check which one I was doing.

Here's the other part. The thing underneath the thing.

When I said *it just wasn't the right fit* I wasn't only performing for Reese. I was also, I think, performing for some version of myself that still needs the clean story. The one where I'm someone who evaluates options and makes considered choices and doesn't apply for jobs at 11pm because something good got close and he got scared. That version is easier to be. He has a clear explanation for everything. He doesn't have to tell anyone, including himself, that he spent two years in a city treating it like a waiting room and then when it finally became somewhere he was choosing to be, his first move was to look for the nearest exit.

I told Avery the messy version. Reese got the clean one.

And I don't think that's exactly wrong — Reese and I are still early, the coffee and the kitchen, the tab left open, we're in the part of knowing someone where you're still deciding what you're allowed to say. I'm not obligated to hand her the full archaeology of my decision-making in an elevator at 6pm. But I noticed the gap. I noticed it in real time and then let the sentence finish anyway, which is a different thing from not noticing it. That's the part I'm trying to look at.

*What do you actually mean* is a harder question than it sounds when the thing you mean requires you to admit you were scared of something you'd been saying you wanted. I've been getting better at the admission in the places where I've already established that admission is allowed. Avery. Nat. Ben, eventually. The ceiling at 10:30, the overdone pasta, the rest stop. I know how to do it in those rooms.

The elevator was a different room and I defaulted.

I'm going to see Reese tomorrow. Same coffee, same bad light, same 9:40 coincidence that happens a few times a week now. And I think what I'm going to do is not a correction exactly — I'm not going to walk in and say *actually, about what I said yesterday* — but I'm going to let it be a little more real than *wasn't the right fit.* If she asks, or if the thread of it comes back up naturally, which it might, I'll give her more of the material. Not the whole essay. Just enough to be the actual version rather than the clean one.

She said *the people who knew you before you knew how to do any of this yet* and she meant it about herself and I recognized it and we've been talking in the bad kitchen light ever since. I think she can handle the version where I applied for something partly out of fear and came back anyway.

I think I can handle saying it.

That's the whole gap, right there. Not the distance between what I said and what I meant. The distance between knowing I can say the real thing and then saying it, versus knowing and saying the other thing anyway because the other thing is already formed and waiting and the real thing requires one more second I didn't take.

The second is getting shorter. That's what I keep noticing.

Tomorrow: bad coffee, the second that got away, try again.

parts.name: The Alarm
parts.description: Sounds whenever a vulnerable truth nears the surface, redirecting the writer back to the managed version before he can finish saying the real thing.

parts.name: The Approval-Seeker
parts.description: The part starved for the nod, the verdict of being worth something, that drives him to volunteer and lean forward before he's checked whether he wants the thing.

parts.name: The Avoider
parts.description: When feelings get too full, this part redirects into distraction — phone scrolling, takeout over the keyboard, crackers at the backsplash, putting things in the pile.

parts.name: The Catastrophizer
parts.description: Runs anxious laps about the future, treating every present moment as a hallway leading to the next deliverable, nod, or verdict.

parts.name: The Critic
parts.description: Tracks failures, runs internal ledgers, and attacks with comparisons and harsh self-judgment.

parts.name: The Hedger
parts.description: Keeps things at arm's length and provisional — the forty-dollar chair, the application as escape door, the wanting kept in the pocket — to avoid committing to a hope that could be lost.

parts.name: The Insufficient One
parts.description: Carries the conviction of being too much, not enough, eventually exhausting to the people who love him.

parts.name: The Lonely Boy
parts.description: Carries the weight of being alone in a new city, scattered from his people, the version of himself before he learned to manage things.

parts.name: The Performer
parts.description: Drafts the smooth, managed version of every interaction to control how the writer is seen.

highlights.exact: I didn't because it was late and also because I've been coming to Avery with a lot of work stuff and I can feel myself pushing against some limit I can't see clearly. Not Avery's limit. Some limit I have about how much I want to need someone. About whether needing them that much is the same as being too much. I do this, I know I do this. I get close to saying the real thing and then I hear some alarm that says *careful* and I put it away.

I know why I did it. I know exactly why I did it and I can barely stand to write it because it's so transparent when I look at it flat: I wanted Drew to think I was someone with capacity. Someone unflappable. Someone who doesn't get in the weeds or make things complicated in the middle, and I know I said that before, I know that's a pattern I keep snapping back to. I wanted to look like more than I feel like right now.

The Avoider.positiveIntent: Stop the buildup. Make the heaviness pause for an hour even if nothing actually gets done.
The Avoider.fearedOutcome: Sitting with a feeling that doesn't have anywhere to go — being trapped inside it without an exit.
The Avoider.whatItProtects: An exhausted part that doesn't believe rest is permitted unless everything is finished, which it never is.
The Avoider.userNotes: Shows up around 9pm with my phone in my hand. The scrolling isn't fun by 10:30 but it's easier than going to bed and admitting the day is over.

The Lonely Boy.positiveIntent: Be safe. Not be the one who didn't do the thing they were supposed to do.
The Lonely Boy.fearedOutcome: Being seen having forgotten — the specific sinking of being called on without the answer.
The Lonely Boy.whatItProtects: (itself — the youngest of the parts, the one the others are organized around keeping out of the room)
The Lonely Boy.userNotes: Surfaces when someone asks something completely benign about whether I did a thing. The grown-up voice answers in a second; this part has already been there for a few seconds.

The Performer.positiveIntent: Keep the day from going off the rails so I can be reliable for the people counting on me.
The Performer.fearedOutcome: Letting something slip and watching someone notice — the small flicker of "oh, you didn't" that I can't un-see.
The Performer.whatItProtects: A quieter part of me that goes blank when too many things are happening at once.
The Performer.userNotes: Loudest on Sunday evenings reviewing the week ahead, and on Monday mornings when the inbox loads. Quieter on Friday afternoons, almost absent on long walks.
